title: Accountants and auditors
rationale: Accounting and auditing are largely computer-based information work: analyzing financial records, preparing reports, checking compliance, and communicating findings. AI can automate many routine and even some analytical tasks, boosting productivity significantly, but human judgment, client trust, regulatory accountability, and licensed sign-off keep the occupation from being fully automatable.

title: Actors
rationale: Acting depends heavily on embodied human performance, physical presence, live collaboration, and audience appeal, which limits direct AI automation of the core job. However, AI can replace or reduce demand in some subfields—such as voiceover, background acting, and digital likeness/performance augmentation—so the occupation faces meaningful but not overwhelming exposure.

title: Actuaries
rationale: Actuarial work is overwhelmingly digital: analyzing data, building models, forecasting risk, pricing policies, and producing reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. The occupation still requires domain-specific judgment, regulatory accountability, and communication with executives and clients, which keeps exposure below the maximum, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and raise productivity.

title: Administrative services and facilities managers
rationale: This job mixes substantial computer-based planning, coordination, records management, policy review, and communication—areas where AI can boost productivity—with important onsite responsibilities like monitoring facilities, supervising maintenance staff, and handling building safety, repairs, and compliance. AI is likely to automate or streamline the administrative and analytical parts of the role, but the physical oversight, vendor coordination, and real-world judgment keep exposure below the highest tier.

title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
rationale: This job combines significant in-person teaching, motivation, and cultural sensitivity with substantial knowledge-work tasks such as lesson planning, assessment, progress monitoring, and individualized instruction. AI can increasingly handle tutoring, translation, practice exercises, and administrative prep, which could materially raise teacher productivity, but the core role still depends on human classroom management, adaptation, and relationship-building.

title: Advertising sales agents
rationale: This job mixes relationship-driven selling with a large amount of digital information work: prospecting, preparing proposals, analyzing accounts, drafting contracts, and creating sales materials are all increasingly AI-automatable. But winning clients still depends heavily on human persuasion, trust, and ongoing account management, especially in live interactions, so AI is more likely to reshape and compress the role than fully replace it.

title: Advertising, promotions, and marketing managers
rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: analyzing markets, developing campaigns, choosing media, pricing strategy, and communicating with clients and staff are all areas where AI can already assist heavily and is improving fast. Human judgment, client relationships, negotiation, and team leadership still matter, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and raise productivity enough to reduce demand for some managerial and supporting roles.

title: Aerospace engineering and operations technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes technical knowledge work with substantial hands-on physical tasks such as building test facilities, installing instruments, calibrating equipment, and supporting manufacturing and assembly. AI can meaningfully assist with simulations, data analysis, troubleshooting, and documentation, but the core work still requires on-site interaction with specialized hardware, safety procedures, and real-world testing environments.

title: Aerospace engineers
rationale: Aerospace engineering is largely computer-based knowledge work—designing, simulating, analyzing, documenting, and evaluating systems—so AI can strongly assist and increase productivity in many core tasks. But the occupation remains constrained by safety-critical judgment, physical testing, regulatory compliance, and accountability for real-world hardware, which makes full automation much harder than for purely digital roles.

title: Agricultural and food science technicians
rationale: This job mixes physical lab, plant, farm, and field work with data analysis and reporting. AI can help interpret test results, automate documentation, and support quality-control decisions, but core tasks like collecting samples, operating equipment, maintaining sterile environments, and inspecting physical materials still require hands-on human presence.

title: Agricultural and food scientists
rationale: Agricultural and food scientists do substantial knowledge work that AI can increasingly assist with, including data analysis, literature review, report writing, communication, and parts of experiment design. But the occupation also depends heavily on laboratory work, field sampling, facility visits, regulatory oversight, and scientific judgment tied to physical biological systems, which limits full automation.

title: Agricultural engineers
rationale: Agricultural engineers do substantial computer-based design, analysis, modeling, and documentation work, so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on field visits, equipment testing, site-specific engineering judgment, and coordination around physical agricultural systems, which limits full automation.

title: Agricultural workers
rationale: Agricultural workers’ core tasks are physical, outdoor, and hands-on: planting, harvesting, feeding animals, cleaning enclosures, and operating machinery in real environments. AI may indirectly reduce labor demand through smarter equipment, robotics, and monitoring systems, but it does not directly automate most day-to-day work from a computer.

title: Air traffic controllers
rationale: Air traffic control involves substantial information processing on screens, and AI can assist with routing, conflict detection, weather integration, and decision support—potentially letting each controller manage more traffic. But the core job is real-time, safety-critical control of aircraft in the physical world under heavy regulation, where human judgment, accountability, and rapid coordination remain major barriers to full automation.

title: Aircraft and avionics equipment mechanics and technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical, hands-on, and safety-critical: inspecting, testing, repairing, and installing aircraft components in hangars and on airfields. AI can help with diagnostics, interpreting manuals and flight-test data, and paperwork, but it does not remove the need for certified technicians to perform and verify repairs in the physical world.

title: Airline and commercial pilots
rationale: Piloting involves significant physical presence, real-time control, safety-critical judgment, and coordination in unpredictable environments, which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. However, aviation is already highly automated, and AI can further reshape flight planning, monitoring, training, and some cockpit decision support, so the occupation faces meaningful but not near-term complete automation.

title: Animal care and service workers
rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical and in-person: feeding, cleaning, grooming, exercising, handling, and training animals all require direct human presence in unpredictable real-world settings. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, recordkeeping, or monitoring animal behavior, but it is unlikely to automate the core hands-on care and service work.

title: Announcers and DJs
rationale: This occupation is split between highly exposed broadcast/podcast work and lower-exposure live event DJ/emcee work. Broadcast announcing is largely digital—researching, writing, recording, editing, and even presenting scripted content—and BLS explicitly notes stations may replace live DJs with AI, but live audience interaction, on-air personality, interviewing, and in-person event hosting still preserve meaningful human value.

title: Anthropologists and archeologists
rationale: This job mixes significant knowledge work—analyzing findings, managing records, writing reports, and advising organizations—with substantial fieldwork, interviews, excavation, and on-site assessment that require physical presence and human judgment. AI will likely boost productivity in research, data analysis, and documentation, but it is less able to replace the core observational, interpretive, and field-based parts of the occupation.

title: Arbitrators, mediators, and conciliators
rationale: This job mixes substantial knowledge work—reviewing documents, applying laws or precedents, drafting agreements, and managing case information—with a core function of live human facilitation in emotionally charged disputes. AI can significantly assist with research, summarization, document preparation, and procedural tasks, but the occupation still depends heavily on interpersonal trust, neutrality, and real-time judgment between conflicting parties.

title: Architects
rationale: Architecture has a large digital core: creating plans in CADD/BIM, producing drawings and specifications, analyzing constraints, and communicating designs are all areas where AI can substantially boost productivity. But the occupation also depends on client relationships, professional judgment, legal responsibility, and physical site visits to verify construction, which limits full automation.

title: Architectural and engineering managers
rationale: This is mostly knowledge and coordination work—planning projects, budgeting, scheduling, supervising technical staff, and communicating across teams—so AI can significantly assist with analysis, documentation, forecasting, and management workflows. But the role also depends heavily on human leadership, accountability, cross-functional coordination, and oversight of real-world engineering, construction, manufacturing, or R&D activities, which limits full automation.

title: Archivists, curators, and museum workers
rationale: This occupation mixes significant knowledge work—cataloging, research, documentation, database management, exhibit planning, and public communications—with substantial physical, hands-on work preserving objects, installing exhibits, and interacting with visitors. AI can strongly assist with archival organization, metadata creation, research, writing, and records management, but the physical stewardship, authentication, conservation, and in-person museum functions limit full automation.

title: Art directors
rationale: Art direction is largely digital creative work: developing visual concepts, reviewing designs, selecting imagery, and communicating with clients and design teams, all in domains where generative AI is advancing quickly. AI can already accelerate concept generation, mood boards, layout exploration, and image production, though human judgment, taste, client management, and leadership still remain important.

title: Assemblers and fabricators
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on assembly in manufacturing plants using tools, machines, and direct manipulation of parts, which limits direct automation by general-purpose AI. However, it has meaningful indirect exposure because AI-enabled robotics, machine vision, and process automation can increasingly handle repetitive assembly and quality-check tasks, reducing labor demand in some settings.

title: Athletes and sports competitors
rationale: The core work of athletes is intensely physical, in-person performance that depends on bodily skill, training, and real-time competition, which AI cannot replace directly. AI may help with peripheral tasks like video analysis, strategy, scouting opponents, and training optimization, but it does not substitute for the athlete’s on-field or on-court role.

title: Athletic trainers
rationale: Athletic trainers’ core work is hands-on and in-person: evaluating injuries, providing emergency care, applying supports, and guiding rehabilitation in real physical settings. AI may help with documentation, recordkeeping, treatment planning support, and compliance paperwork, but it is unlikely to replace the direct physical care, on-site judgment, and interpersonal coordination central to the job.

title: Atmospheric scientists, including meteorologists
rationale: This occupation is heavily exposed because much of the core work is digital: analyzing large datasets, running models, writing code, generating forecasts, graphics, and reports. AI can significantly automate and accelerate these tasks, though human judgment, scientific interpretation, and responsibility for high-stakes warnings and public communication keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Audiologists
rationale: Audiologists combine diagnostic analysis with substantial in-person clinical work: examining patients, administering hearing and balance tests, fitting devices, and counseling patients and families. AI can meaningfully assist with interpreting test data, documentation, and treatment planning, but the occupation’s core hands-on care, patient interaction, and licensed clinical judgment limit full automation.

title: Automotive body and glass repairers
rationale: This job is centered on physical, hands-on repair work: removing and fitting parts, welding, aligning frames, installing glass, and working directly on vehicles in shops or in the field. AI may help with peripheral tasks like damage estimating, repair documentation, or technical lookup, but it does not meaningfully automate the core manual work requiring dexterity, physical presence, and tool use.

title: Automotive service technicians and mechanics
rationale: This job’s core work is physical: inspecting vehicles, using tools, replacing parts, and working in a repair shop on real machines. AI can assist with diagnostics, repair documentation, and troubleshooting support, but it cannot easily replace the hands-on mechanical work, physical dexterity, and in-person customer interaction that define the occupation.

title: Bakers
rationale: Bakers’ core work is physical and hands-on: measuring ingredients, shaping dough, operating ovens, monitoring batches, cleaning equipment, and often serving customers in person. AI may help at the margins with inventory, scheduling, demand forecasting, or recipe standardization, but it does not substantially automate the central manual, on-site tasks of baking.

title: Barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists
rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical and in-person: cutting, coloring, styling, shaving, and interacting directly with clients. AI may help with peripheral tasks like appointment scheduling, marketing, recordkeeping, or hairstyle visualization, but it does not meaningfully automate the hands-on service itself.

title: Bartenders
rationale: Bartending is centered on physical, in-person service: mixing and serving drinks, checking IDs, monitoring intoxication, handling payments, cleaning, and managing a live social environment. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like inventory, scheduling, or POS systems, but it does not substantially automate the core hands-on and interpersonal work.

title: Bill and account collectors
rationale: This job is largely computer-and-phone-based, with routine digital tasks such as locating debtors, reviewing account information, making scripted outreach, documenting interactions, and negotiating repayment plans. AI and automation can already handle or heavily assist much of this workflow—especially calling, recordkeeping, compliance scripting, and prioritization—though difficult negotiations and handling emotionally charged conversations still leave some role for humans.

title: Biochemists and biophysicists
rationale: AI can meaningfully accelerate major parts of this job, including literature review, data analysis, protein modeling, report writing, and grant drafting. But the occupation still depends heavily on physical laboratory experiments, interdisciplinary teamwork, and high-level scientific judgment in novel research, which limits full automation.

title: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers
rationale: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers do substantial computer-based work—modeling, simulation, software, analysis, documentation, and research—where AI can materially boost productivity and automate parts of design and reporting. But the occupation also depends heavily on physical device development, experimentation, regulatory/safety validation, and collaboration with clinicians, manufacturers, and labs, which limits full automation. AI is likely to significantly reshape the job, but not replace the hands-on engineering and real-world testing core.

title: Biological technicians
rationale: Biological technicians have meaningful AI exposure in the digital parts of the job—data analysis, recordkeeping, report writing, and some interpretation of results. But much of the core work still requires physical lab presence, sample handling, equipment setup, experiment execution, and field collection, which limits full automation.

title: Boilermakers
rationale: Boilermaking is overwhelmingly physical, on-site work involving welding, fitting, inspecting, and repairing large equipment in cramped, hazardous, and unpredictable environments. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, diagnostics, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on trade work.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, centered on entering, classifying, reconciling, and checking financial data in software, spreadsheets, and databases—exactly the kind of routine information processing AI and automation can increasingly perform. BLS already notes software is reducing demand and shifting remaining workers toward more analytical and advisory tasks, indicating major restructuring even if some human oversight and client communication remain.

title: Broadcast, sound, and video technicians
rationale: This job mixes substantial hands-on physical work—setting up, operating, repairing, and dismantling equipment on site—with digital tasks such as recording, editing, mixing, monitoring, and format conversion. AI can increasingly automate parts of the digital production and control workflow and raise each technician’s productivity, but live-event support, installation, troubleshooting, and physical equipment handling keep exposure below fully digital occupations.

title: Budget analysts
rationale: Budget analysts do predominantly digital knowledge work: reviewing proposals, analyzing financial data, preparing reports, forecasting needs, and writing recommendations. AI can automate or heavily assist much of this analysis, summarization, compliance checking, and report drafting, though human judgment, organizational context, and explaining recommendations to managers or legislators keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Bus drivers
rationale: Bus driving is centered on physical, real-world vehicle operation, passenger safety, and in-person assistance, which limits near-term AI automation compared with fully digital jobs. However, AI and autonomy could meaningfully reshape the occupation over time—especially on fixed routes through driver-assistance systems, scheduling, dispatch, fare handling, and eventually some forms of autonomous transit—so exposure is moderate rather than low.

title: Butchers
rationale: Butchers’ core work is highly physical and hands-on: cutting, deboning, packaging meat, handling tools, maintaining sanitation, and serving customers in person. AI may help only at the margins with inventory tracking, ordering, and sales analysis, but it does little to automate the central manual and on-site tasks.

title: Calibration technologists and technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on: inspecting, adjusting, testing, and maintaining physical measurement devices in labs, plants, and other on-site settings. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, diagnostics, and analyzing test data, but it does not remove the need for physical manipulation, precision adjustment, and real-world verification against standards.

title: Cardiovascular technologists and technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on and patient-facing: preparing and positioning patients, operating diagnostic equipment, assisting in procedures, and monitoring patients in real time in clinical settings. AI can help with interpreting test data, flagging abnormalities, and documentation, but it is unlikely to replace the physical presence, technical operation, and direct teamwork with physicians that define the occupation.

title: Career and technical education teachers
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, creating instructional materials, grading, and some tutoring or content delivery. But career and technical education teachers still rely heavily on in-person instruction, hands-on demonstrations, classroom management, and supervision of tools, equipment, and safety procedures, which limits full automation.

title: Carpenters
rationale: Carpentry is overwhelmingly physical, hands-on work performed on construction sites using tools, materials, and real-time adaptation to specific conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like estimating, blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or calculations, but it does not substantially automate the core manual installation, repair, and construction work.

title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
rationale: This occupation is largely digital knowledge work: analyzing geospatial data, integrating GIS/CAD inputs, and producing maps and visualizations on a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image interpretation, feature extraction, data fusion, and automated map generation, though human judgment, quality control, and occasional field verification still limit full automation.

title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashiers have significant AI exposure because much of the core job is routine transaction processing that can be automated through self-checkout, computer vision, digital payments, and retail automation systems. The role still requires physical presence and customer interaction, which prevents maximum exposure, but AI and adjacent automation are likely to keep reducing headcount and reshaping the job substantially.

title: Chefs and head cooks
rationale: Chefs and head cooks do highly physical, on-site work centered on cooking, tasting, supervising kitchens, and managing real-time food service operations, which limits direct automation by AI. AI can assist with peripheral tasks like menu drafting, inventory, scheduling, costing, and marketing, but it does not replace the core hands-on culinary and leadership work.

title: Chemical engineers
rationale: Chemical engineers do substantial knowledge work on computers—process design, analysis, simulation, research, compliance, and documentation—so AI can meaningfully assist and raise productivity. But the occupation also depends on plant operations, lab testing, safety oversight, regulatory judgment, and troubleshooting in physical manufacturing environments, which limits full automation.

title: Chemical technicians
rationale: Chemical technicians do substantial hands-on physical work in labs and manufacturing settings: setting up instruments, preparing solutions, monitoring processes, maintaining equipment, and running experiments all require onsite presence. AI can meaningfully assist with data analysis, interpreting results, documentation, reporting, and troubleshooting support, but it does not eliminate the core lab-based and process-monitoring duties.

title: Chemists and materials scientists
rationale: AI is likely to significantly reshape the analytical and digital parts of this job—literature review, modeling, simulation, experimental design support, data analysis, and technical writing. But much of the occupation still depends on physical laboratory work, hands-on experimentation, safety procedures, and real-world validation, which limits full automation.

title: Childcare workers
rationale: Childcare work is centered on physical presence, hands-on care, safety monitoring, and real-time interaction with children in unpredictable environments, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like recordkeeping, scheduling, activity planning, or parent communication, but it does not meaningfully replace the core work.

title: Chiropractors
rationale: Chiropractors’ core work is hands-on and in-person: examining patients, performing spinal and joint adjustments, and building trust with people in pain. AI may assist with documentation, scheduling, imaging interpretation, and practice management, but it is unlikely to replace the manual treatment and physical patient interaction that define the occupation.

title: Civil engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes digital design, drafting, cost estimating, data analysis, and reporting—tasks where AI can significantly boost productivity—with field surveys, site inspections, materials testing, and construction observation that still require physical presence. AI is likely to automate or accelerate the office-based support work, but the occupation is not fully digital because core responsibilities remain tied to real-world infrastructure projects.

title: Civil engineers
rationale: Civil engineers do substantial computer-based work—analyzing data, producing designs in CAD, estimating costs, preparing permits, and reviewing compliance—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends heavily on site conditions, construction oversight, coordination with stakeholders, and licensed human judgment tied to safety and legal responsibility, which limits full automation.

title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
rationale: This occupation combines substantial information-processing work—reviewing documents, evaluating coverage, estimating losses, detecting fraud, and authorizing payments—with some field inspection, interviewing, and negotiation. AI is already well suited to photo-based damage assessment, claims triage, document review, and anomaly detection, and BLS explicitly expects automation and AI to raise productivity and reduce employment, though physical inspections and contested cases still require human judgment.

title: Clinical laboratory technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes significant hands-on physical lab work—handling specimens, operating and maintaining equipment, following safety protocols, and sometimes interacting with patients—with information-processing tasks like analyzing results and documenting them. AI and automation can increasingly assist with image analysis, pattern detection, quality control, and routine testing workflows, but the need for physical specimen handling, regulated procedures, and on-site laboratory presence limits full automation.

title: Coaches and scouts
rationale: AI can assist coaches and scouts with video analysis, performance data, opponent scouting, recruiting research, and training-plan support. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person instruction, live game decisions, athlete motivation, relationship-building, and physical presence at practices and competitions, which limits overall exposure.

title: Community health workers
rationale: Community health workers spend much of their time in the field doing outreach, home visits, coaching, advocacy, and culturally sensitive interpersonal support—core tasks that require human trust and physical presence. AI can help with documentation, translation, resource matching, and case-management logistics, but it is unlikely to replace the relationship-based, community-embedded nature of the work.

title: Compensation and benefits managers
rationale: This is predominantly computer-based knowledge work: analyzing compensation data, monitoring regulations, preparing budgets, evaluating vendors, and communicating recommendations are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the job’s managerial, strategic, and compliance-heavy nature, which still requires human judgment, cross-functional coordination, and accountability for sensitive pay and benefits decisions.

title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
rationale: This occupation is predominantly computer-based knowledge work: researching policies, analyzing compensation data, evaluating job descriptions, preparing reports, and monitoring regulations. AI can substantially automate and accelerate these digital tasks, though human judgment, legal accountability, and stakeholder communication still matter enough to keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Compliance officers
rationale: Compliance officers do predominantly computer-based knowledge work: reading regulations, analyzing risks, auditing records, writing policies and reports, and answering compliance questions. AI is well suited to summarizing rules, monitoring documents, drafting guidance, and flagging issues, though human judgment, accountability, investigations, and stakeholder interaction keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Computer and information research scientists
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, coding, experimenting, analyzing results, and writing papers can largely be done on a computer, so AI is positioned to affect nearly every core task. Although frontier research, novel problem formulation, and interdisciplinary judgment still require substantial human expertise, AI is likely to sharply increase productivity and restructure how this work is done.

title: Computer and information systems managers
rationale: This is highly digital knowledge work: analyzing IT needs, evaluating projects, coordinating technical teams, and communicating plans are all areas where AI can significantly augment productivity. But the role also depends heavily on leadership, organizational judgment, vendor negotiation, and accountability for business-critical decisions, which makes full automation less likely than for purely individual contributor digital jobs.

title: Computer hardware engineers
rationale: Computer hardware engineering involves substantial digital knowledge work—designing schematics, analyzing test results, simulating circuits, and coordinating with software—so AI can significantly accelerate core parts of the job. But the occupation is still constrained by physical-world requirements like hardware testing, manufacturing oversight, and real-world performance validation, which limit full automation.

title: Computer network architects
rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: designing network layouts, analyzing performance, documenting systems, recommending technologies, and troubleshooting are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is somewhat tempered by the need for high-stakes judgment, security considerations, coordination with teams and vendors, and occasional hands-on deployment with hardware.

title: Computer programmers
rationale: Computer programming is almost entirely digital work done on a computer: writing, modifying, testing, and debugging code are all tasks where AI is advancing rapidly. AI can already automate substantial portions of routine coding and error-fixing, and the BLS description itself notes automation and AI reducing demand, though human oversight, system understanding, and collaboration keep exposure just below the absolute maximum.

title: Computer support specialists
rationale: Computer support specialists do predominantly digital work—diagnosing issues, guiding users, documenting problems, and troubleshooting systems over phone, chat, email, or remote tools—so AI can automate or heavily assist much of the core workflow. Some exposure is limited by the need for hands-on device setup, onsite support, and complex real-time customer interaction, but the occupation is already being reshaped by chatbots and automated troubleshooting.

title: Computer systems analysts
rationale: This is highly digital knowledge work: analyzing systems, researching technologies, modeling processes, writing documentation, and sometimes coding are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the need for business context, stakeholder consultation, cross-team coordination, and judgment about organizational tradeoffs, which still leave an important human role.

title: Concierges
rationale: Concierge work has meaningful AI exposure because many core tasks—answering routine questions, making reservations, handling messages, and coordinating services—can be assisted or partly automated by apps and AI agents. But the job still depends heavily on physical presence, real-time customer service, trust, and handling in-person requests in hotels, residences, and offices, which limits full automation.

title: Conservation scientists and foresters
rationale: This job mixes meaningful knowledge work—analyzing environmental data, GIS and remote sensing, planning, reporting, and contracts—with substantial fieldwork, physical presence, and on-the-ground judgment in forests, rangelands, and fire conditions. AI will likely improve productivity on mapping, monitoring, and analysis tasks, but it cannot replace much of the core in-person inspection, land management, emergency response, and stakeholder coordination.

title: Construction and building inspectors
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and site-based: inspecting structures, using testing equipment, climbing through spaces, and verifying real-world conditions against code. AI can help with plan review, report writing, code lookup, scheduling, and some remote inspection workflows, but it cannot replace most onsite inspection duties, so exposure is moderate rather than high.

title: Construction equipment operators
rationale: This job is centered on operating heavy machinery in physical, dynamic construction environments, with real-time spatial judgment, safety awareness, and on-site coordination that AI alone cannot easily replace. AI may increasingly assist through GPS guidance, automation features, diagnostics, and planning, but the core work still requires human presence and control of equipment in the field.

title: Construction laborers and helpers
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-based: moving materials, cleaning sites, digging, setting up scaffolding, and assisting tradesworkers in real-world environments. AI may modestly affect planning, equipment, or prefabrication around the edges, but it does not directly automate most core daily tasks without broader advances in robotics.

title: Construction managers
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with estimating, budgeting, scheduling, contract review, progress reporting, and other information-heavy parts of the job. But construction managers still spend much of their time onsite coordinating people, handling delays and emergencies, and making real-world judgment calls in changing physical environments, which limits full automation.

title: Cooks
rationale: Cooking is fundamentally physical, hands-on work that requires on-site food preparation, kitchen coordination, and sensory judgment, which creates a strong barrier to direct AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like menu planning, inventory, scheduling, and some fast-food process automation, but it does not substantially replace the core work of most cooks.

title: Correctional officers and bailiffs
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, in-person security and supervision in prisons and courtrooms: maintaining order, escorting people, conducting searches, and responding to unpredictable confrontations. AI may assist with paperwork, surveillance review, risk flagging, and scheduling, but it does not replace the need for constant human presence, authority, and physical intervention.

title: Cost estimators
rationale: Cost estimating is largely digital knowledge work: analyzing data, reading technical documents, preparing bids, and writing reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Some human judgment and occasional site visits remain important, but improving estimation software and the occupation’s already declining employment outlook indicate substantial AI-driven productivity gains and restructuring.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
rationale: This occupation’s core output is fully digital text generated from speech, and AI speech recognition, real-time transcription, and captioning tools are improving rapidly and already automate large portions of the work. Human workers still matter for legal-grade accuracy, speaker identification, clarification, and quality control in high-stakes settings, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped by AI through automation and reduced labor needed per transcript.

title: Craft and fine artists
rationale: AI can strongly affect the digital parts of this occupation—especially illustration, concept generation, portfolio creation, marketing copy, and online sales—while also increasing competition through cheap AI-generated images. But much of the occupation remains rooted in physical making, distinctive personal style, hands-on materials, and the human value buyers place on originality and craft, which limits full automation.

title: Credit counselors
rationale: Credit counselors do substantial digital information work—analyzing finances, calculating budgets, generating debt plans, and preparing written recommendations—all areas where AI can significantly assist or partially automate. But the job also depends heavily on trust, empathy, client communication, and negotiation with creditors, so AI is more likely to augment and reduce headcount somewhat than fully replace the human role.

title: Customer service representatives
rationale: This job is highly exposed because much of the core work—answering questions, handling routine requests, processing account changes, documenting interactions, and communicating by phone/chat/email—is digital and increasingly automatable by AI. Some roles still require human judgment, empathy, or in-person help for complex and upset customers, but the occupation is already being reduced by self-service systems and AI will likely further shrink headcount by handling a growing share of standard interactions.

title: Dancers and choreographers
rationale: The core of this occupation is embodied physical performance, rehearsal, and in-person artistic direction, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like music selection, promotion, budgeting, or generating choreographic ideas, but it is unlikely to replace live dancers’ physical skill or choreographers’ hands-on instruction and staging in the near term.

title: Data scientists
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: collecting, cleaning, analyzing data, building models, writing code, creating visualizations, and communicating findings are all tasks where AI is advancing rapidly. Human judgment, stakeholder communication, and domain context still matter, but AI is likely to dramatically increase each data scientist’s productivity and reshape the role.

title: Database administrators and architects
rationale: This is highly digital, computer-based work: designing schemas, writing/querying SQL, monitoring performance, troubleshooting, documenting, and managing permissions are all tasks where AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But the role still carries significant responsibility for security, reliability, production risk, and organization-specific architecture decisions, so AI is more likely to reshape and boost productivity than fully replace the occupation.

title: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers
rationale: This job is centered on physical driving, loading/unloading, and in-person delivery in unpredictable real-world environments, which limits near-term AI automation. But AI can already optimize routing, dispatching, customer communication, and paperwork, and over time advances in autonomous vehicles and delivery systems could significantly reshape staffing and productivity.

title: Dental and ophthalmic laboratory technicians and medical appliance technicians
rationale: This work is centered on physical fabrication, repair, finishing, and quality inspection of medical devices, dentures, and lenses, which limits direct AI automation because it requires hands-on dexterity, specialized materials handling, and use of tools and machinery. AI and related automation can still materially reshape the occupation through CAD software, 3D printing, and automated lens or dental part production, increasing productivity and reducing labor needs in some specialties.

title: Dental assistants
rationale: Dental assistants’ core work is hands-on and in-person: preparing treatment rooms, sterilizing instruments, assisting during procedures, taking x rays, and supporting patients in real time. AI may reduce time spent on scheduling, billing, records, and some imaging/documentation tasks, but it does not replace the physical chairside assistance and patient interaction that define the job.

title: Dental hygienists
rationale: Dental hygienists’ core work is hands-on, physical, and performed in person: cleaning teeth, applying treatments, taking x-rays, and working precisely inside patients’ mouths. AI may help with documentation, imaging interpretation, and care planning, but it does not replace the occupation’s central manual and patient-facing tasks.

title: Dentists
rationale: Dentistry has meaningful AI exposure in diagnostic imaging, treatment planning, documentation, scheduling, and practice management, where AI can improve productivity. But the core job is hands-on physical care in a patient’s mouth requiring dexterity, licensure, and in-person patient interaction, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Desktop publishers
rationale: Desktop publishing is almost entirely digital work done on a computer: formatting text, integrating graphics, revising layouts, and preparing files for print or online publication. AI can increasingly automate or accelerate core tasks such as layout generation, proofreading, image editing, and template-based design, likely reducing headcount even where human aesthetic judgment and collaboration still matter.

title: Diagnostic medical sonographers
rationale: This job’s core work requires in-person patient care, physical positioning, hands-on operation of ultrasound equipment, and real-time adjustment of the scan based on what is seen, which creates a strong barrier to full AI automation. AI can meaningfully assist with image quality checks, abnormality detection, measurements, documentation, and summaries, but it is more likely to augment sonographers than replace most of the role.

title: Diesel service technicians and mechanics
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on inspection, repair, and maintenance of diesel vehicles in shops or at worksites, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI can help with diagnostics, interpreting manuals, and administrative tasks, but it does not replace the manual dexterity, troubleshooting in the physical world, and in-person testing that define the occupation.

title: Dietitians and nutritionists
rationale: Dietitians and nutritionists do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: analyzing dietary information, drafting meal plans, creating educational materials, summarizing research, and documenting client progress. But the occupation also depends on licensed clinical judgment, individualized counseling, empathy, and coordination with patients and care teams, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Drafters
rationale: Drafters' core work is highly digital: translating sketches and specifications into CAD/BIM technical drawings on a computer. That makes the occupation very exposed to AI-driven automation and productivity gains, especially because generative design tools can create, revise, and detail plans, and BLS already notes that software lets engineers and architects absorb tasks once done by drafters.

title: Drywall installers, ceiling tile installers, and tapers
rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical and on-site: measuring, cutting, lifting, fastening, taping, and finishing drywall in real buildings. AI may modestly help with estimating materials, scheduling, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Economists
rationale: Economists do predominantly digital knowledge work: collecting and analyzing data, building models, forecasting trends, and writing reports and presentations. AI is rapidly improving at statistical analysis, coding, summarization, and drafting, so it can automate or accelerate large parts of the job, though higher-level judgment, policy interpretation, and credibility with decision-makers still keep humans central.

title: Editors
rationale: Editing is overwhelmingly digital work: reading, rewriting, proofreading, fact-checking, headline generation, and coordinating written content are all tasks AI can already assist with and increasingly automate. Human judgment, taste, and editorial decision-making still matter, especially for ethics and publication choices, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped as AI raises productivity and reduces demand for routine editing work.

title: Electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes hands-on physical work—assembling prototypes, calibrating instruments, testing equipment, repairing components, and visiting sites—with technical analysis and documentation. AI can significantly assist with diagnostics, test-data analysis, drafting diagrams/specifications, and reporting, but much of the core work still requires physical presence, lab equipment, and manual troubleshooting.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
rationale: This is largely knowledge work done with CAD, simulation, analysis, programming, and documentation, so AI can significantly assist or automate parts of design iteration, troubleshooting, and technical writing. But the job still depends heavily on domain judgment, safety-critical validation, hardware testing, and coordination with manufacturing and physical systems, which limits full automation.

title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
rationale: This job is centered on physical, on-site installation, testing, disassembly, and repair of electrical and electronic equipment, which limits direct AI automation. AI can help with diagnostics, documentation, and troubleshooting support, but the core work still requires hands-on skill, use of tools, and presence in factories, substations, vehicles, or other job sites.

title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, outdoor, and safety-critical: climbing poles, operating equipment, handling high-voltage lines, and restoring service in unpredictable field conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like outage analysis, diagnostics, and planning, but it does not substantially automate the hands-on repair and installation work.

title: Electricians
rationale: Electricians’ core work is hands-on and site-specific: installing, troubleshooting, and repairing physical electrical systems in homes, businesses, and construction environments. AI may help with peripheral tasks like reading plans, diagnostics support, code lookup, or scheduling, but it does not replace the manual skill, safety judgment, and physical presence the job requires.

title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes technical knowledge work with substantial hands-on physical work: assembling, testing, calibrating, repairing, and maintaining electromechanical and robotic equipment on-site. AI can assist with diagnostics, documentation, test analysis, and troubleshooting, but the core tasks still require physical presence, manual skill, and interaction with real machinery.

title: Elementary, middle, and high school principals
rationale: School principals do substantial knowledge work—analyzing achievement data, writing reports, managing schedules and budgets, and coordinating staff—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity on many administrative tasks. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person leadership, student discipline, teacher observation, crisis handling, and trust-based relationships with parents, staff, and the community, which limits full automation.

title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, on-site installation, maintenance, and repair of complex equipment in shafts, machine rooms, and other real-world environments. AI may help with diagnostics, reading documentation, and service records, but it is unlikely to replace the hands-on mechanical, electrical, and safety-critical work that defines the occupation.

title: Emergency management directors
rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: drafting emergency plans, analyzing hazards and damage assessments, reviewing regulations, preparing grant applications, and coordinating information. But core responsibilities also depend heavily on real-world leadership, interagency coordination, public communication, and high-stakes decision-making during live emergencies, which require human presence and judgment.

title: EMTs and paramedics
rationale: EMTs and paramedics do highly physical, real-time emergency work that requires on-scene presence, patient handling, transport, and rapid interpersonal judgment in unpredictable environments. AI may help with documentation, dispatch, decision support, and interpreting data like EKGs, but it is unlikely to replace the core hands-on field care and transport functions.

title: Entertainment and recreation managers
rationale: This job mixes office-based planning, budgeting, scheduling, and staff coordination—areas where AI can provide meaningful assistance—with substantial on-site operational oversight, customer service, and responsibility for facility safety. AI will likely boost productivity in the administrative and analytical parts of the role, but the core job still depends heavily on in-person management, judgment, and real-world coordination.

title: Environmental engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This job combines meaningful digital and analytical tasks—recordkeeping, document review, sample analysis, scheduling, and compliance work—with substantial hands-on field and lab work that requires physical presence. AI and automation can boost productivity in testing, reporting, and monitoring, but they are less able to replace on-site sampling, equipment handling, inspections, and hazardous-material coordination.

title: Environmental engineers
rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial computer-based knowledge work—analyzing data, preparing reports, reviewing permits, and designing pollution-control or water-treatment systems—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on site inspections, project monitoring, regulatory accountability, and engineering judgment tied to physical infrastructure, which limits full automation.

title: Environmental science and protection technicians
rationale: This job mixes field inspections, physical sample collection, equipment setup, and lab handling with some digital reporting and analysis. AI can assist with documentation, data interpretation, and compliance support, but the core work still depends heavily on on-site presence, precise physical procedures, and human judgment in real-world environments.

title: Environmental scientists and specialists
rationale: This job has substantial knowledge-work components—analyzing environmental data, preparing reports, assessing risks, and advising clients or officials—that AI can increasingly assist with. But core work still often depends on field sampling, laboratory activity, regulatory interpretation, and context-specific scientific judgment, so AI is more likely to augment than fully replace these workers.

title: Epidemiologists
rationale: Epidiology is heavily exposed because much of the core work is digital knowledge work: analyzing datasets, designing studies, writing reports, communicating findings, and drafting grant proposals. But exposure is not at the very top because the job also depends on domain judgment, public health decision-making, fieldwork, handling samples, and coordination during real-world health investigations and emergencies.

title: Exercise physiologists
rationale: Exercise physiologists do use information-heavy tasks that AI can support, such as analyzing medical histories, interpreting test data, documenting progress, and suggesting personalized exercise plans. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person assessment, monitoring patients during exercise, hands-on implementation, and human judgment and rapport in healthcare settings, which limits overall exposure.

title: Farmers, ranchers, and other agricultural managers
rationale: This occupation is grounded in physical, on-site work: supervising planting, harvesting, livestock care, equipment, land, and facilities all require real-world presence and hands-on judgment. AI can meaningfully assist with planning, forecasting, recordkeeping, pricing, and operational decisions, but it does not replace the core physical and environmental management of running a farm or ranch.

title: Fashion designers
rationale: Fashion design has a large digital and conceptual component—sketching, CAD work, trend research, ideation, presentation, and marketing—that AI can increasingly generate or accelerate. But the occupation is not fully automatable because material selection, fit on real bodies, prototype iteration, production oversight, and brand-specific creative judgment still require substantial human involvement.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: AI exposure is very high overall because film and video editing is almost entirely digital, and AI can already assist with organizing footage, rough cuts, color/audio cleanup, effects, and content generation. Exposure is somewhat tempered by camera-operator work, which still requires physical presence, equipment handling, and creative on-set judgment, but robotic cameras and workflow automation will still reduce labor needs and reshape the occupation.

title: Financial analysts
rationale: Financial analysts do predominantly digital work: analyzing financial data, building forecasts, valuing companies, monitoring trends, and writing reports, all of which are areas where AI is advancing quickly. Human judgment, client trust, and meetings with company officials still matter, but AI is likely to automate substantial portions of research and modeling and significantly increase each analyst’s productivity.

title: Financial clerks
rationale: Financial clerks do largely routine, rules-based information processing: updating records, calculating charges, verifying applications, handling transactions, and communicating standard information. Those tasks are highly digitizable and already being reduced by automation, online self-service, and productivity software, though some roles still require in-person customer service or physical cash handling.

title: Financial examiners
rationale: Financial examiners do predominantly digital knowledge work: reviewing financial statements and loan files, analyzing regulatory impacts, assessing risk, and writing compliance reports. AI can increasingly automate document review, anomaly detection, risk analysis, and report drafting, though human judgment, accountability, and some onsite examination of institutions keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Financial managers
rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: analyzing financial data, preparing reports and forecasts, monitoring compliance, and advising executives are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the need for high-stakes judgment, regulatory accountability, industry-specific expertise, and management of people and organizational decisions.

title: Fire inspectors
rationale: Fire inspectors’ core work depends heavily on physical site visits, examining buildings or fire scenes, handling evidence, and interacting with property owners, witnesses, and courts. AI can help with report writing, code lookup, blueprint review, documentation, and some analytical tasks, but it cannot replace the on-the-ground inspection, evidence collection, and enforcement functions that define the job.

title: Firefighters
rationale: Firefighting is overwhelmingly physical, on-site emergency response work in dangerous, unpredictable environments that require human strength, mobility, and real-time judgment. AI may help at the margins with dispatch support, reporting, training, and risk prediction, but it does not meaningfully automate the core tasks of rescuing people, operating equipment, and responding to fires and medical emergencies.

title: Fishing and hunting workers
rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoors, and done in hazardous real-world environments where workers must operate vessels, handle gear, and catch, sort, and store animals by hand. AI may modestly assist with navigation, fish-finding, weather analysis, or regulatory paperwork, but it does not substantially automate the core hands-on work.

title: Fitness trainers and instructors
rationale: The core of this job is physical, in-person, and interpersonal: demonstrating exercises, watching form, motivating clients, ensuring safety, and providing hands-on coaching. AI can assist with workout planning, progress tracking, marketing, and online content, but it does not replace the real-time human presence central to most training and instruction.

title: Flight attendants
rationale: Flight attendants’ core work is physical and in-person: ensuring passenger safety, responding to emergencies, enforcing regulations, and providing onboard service in a live aircraft environment. AI may automate peripheral tasks such as reporting, scheduling, or some customer communication, but it cannot replace the legal, physical, and interpersonal presence required onboard.

title: Flooring installers and tile and stone setters
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: removing old materials, measuring, cutting, fitting, fastening, grouting, sanding, and finishing in real-world environments. AI may help a bit with estimating materials, scheduling, or reading design plans, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on installation work.

title: Floral designers
rationale: Floral design’s core work is physical and hands-on: selecting fresh materials, arranging flowers, preparing orders, and setting up displays or event installations in the real world. AI may help with peripheral tasks like marketing, order intake, inventory, and suggesting design ideas, but it does not substantially automate the central manual, aesthetic, and customer-facing parts of the job.

title: Food and beverage serving and related workers
rationale: This work is dominated by in-person physical tasks: taking and delivering orders, preparing simple items, cleaning tables, stocking stations, and interacting with customers on site. AI may reduce some demand at the margins through self-order kiosks, reservation systems, and automated payment/order handling, but it does not directly replace most core duties without broader robotics adoption.

title: Food preparation workers
rationale: This job is centered on physical, in-person kitchen tasks like chopping, cleaning, stocking, and handling food safely, which are not easily automated by general AI software. AI may have small indirect effects through ordering, scheduling, or greater use of preprocessed ingredients and kitchen automation, but it does not strongly affect the core daily work.

title: Food processing equipment workers
rationale: This job is centered on physically operating, loading, monitoring, and cleaning food-processing machinery in industrial facilities, so the core work requires on-site presence and hands-on interaction with equipment and materials. AI may improve process control, quality monitoring, and reduce staffing at the margins through smarter automation, but it does not directly replace most daily tasks the way it can in fully digital occupations.

title: Food service managers
rationale: Food service managers have meaningful exposure because AI can help with scheduling, inventory ordering, budgeting, payroll, forecasting, promotions, and some customer-service tasks. But the core job still depends heavily on on-site physical oversight of staff, food safety, kitchen operations, and real-time handling of customers and problems in a restaurant environment, which limits overall exposure.

title: Forensic science technicians
rationale: This job has meaningful AI exposure in its analytical and documentation tasks: AI can assist with image comparison, pattern matching, statistical interpretation, database searches, and drafting reports. But a large share of the occupation still requires physical crime-scene work, evidence handling, lab procedures, chain-of-custody responsibility, and court testimony, which limits full automation.

title: Forest and conservation workers
rationale: This job is dominated by outdoor physical labor in remote, unpredictable environments: planting, clearing brush, operating equipment, spraying vegetation, and helping suppress fires. AI may reduce some employment through remote sensing and automation of selected forestry tasks, but it has limited impact on the core hands-on work that requires human presence and physical stamina.

title: Fundraisers
rationale: Fundraising combines highly AI-exposed digital tasks—researching donors, drafting messages, analyzing campaign results, managing records, and running online outreach—with relationship-driven work that still benefits from human trust and persuasion. AI is likely to make fundraisers significantly more productive and reduce labor needed for routine prospecting and communications, but in-person donor cultivation and event-based fundraising keep exposure below the highest tier.

title: Funeral service workers
rationale: AI can help with administrative and digital tasks such as filing documents, writing obituaries, scheduling services, handling marketing, and assisting with benefits or insurance paperwork. But the core of the occupation depends heavily on in-person grief counseling, coordinating physical logistics, and preparing and handling remains, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Gambling services workers
rationale: Most gambling services work is in-person and physical: dealing games, taking bets, monitoring customers, paying winnings, and supervising casino floors all require real-time human presence and customer interaction. AI may reduce headcount indirectly through online gambling, automated betting, surveillance, and administrative tools, but it does not readily replace the core on-floor service work in physical casinos.

title: General maintenance and repair workers
rationale: This job is centered on hands-on physical repair, inspection, and maintenance in buildings and around machinery, often requiring movement through real-world environments, tool use, and on-site troubleshooting. AI may help with peripheral tasks like diagnostics, recordkeeping, estimating, and ordering supplies, but it does not replace the core manual and in-person work.

title: General office clerks
rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because many core tasks—data entry, document preparation, filing, scheduling, answering routine inquiries, and processing office paperwork—are routine information-processing activities that AI and related software can automate or greatly accelerate. Some in-person duties remain, such as receiving visitors and handling physical mail, but the role is still likely to be heavily restructured and reduced as AI boosts productivity and shifts clerical work to other employees.

title: Genetic counselors
rationale: Genetic counselors do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: collecting histories, reviewing records, interpreting test results, researching conditions, and drafting documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on high-stakes clinical judgment, patient-specific communication, coordination with providers, and emotional support for distressed clients, which limits full automation.

title: Geographers
rationale: Geographers do a large share of their core work on computers: analyzing spatial data, using GIS, creating maps, linking datasets, and writing reports—all areas where AI is advancing quickly. Fieldwork, interviews, and expert judgment about geographic methods and interpretation reduce full automation, but AI is likely to substantially reshape and increase productivity in the occupation.

title: Geological and hydrologic technicians
rationale: This occupation mixes fieldwork, lab work, and office-based analysis. AI can increasingly help with data analysis, mapping, report writing, and interpreting measurements, but substantial core tasks still require physical sample collection, equipment installation and maintenance, and on-site monitoring in remote environments.

title: Geoscientists
rationale: Geoscientists have meaningful AI exposure because parts of the job involve analyzing data, interpreting imagery, using GIS/modeling software, and writing reports—all areas where AI can boost productivity. But the occupation also depends heavily on fieldwork, sample collection, laboratory testing, and physical presence in varied environments, which limits how much AI can replace the core role.

title: Glaziers
rationale: Glaziers’ core work is highly physical and site-specific: measuring, cutting, lifting, positioning, and securing glass in buildings, often at heights and in changing jobsite conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or estimating, but it does little to automate the hands-on installation work itself.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design is overwhelmingly digital work: creating layouts, images, and visual concepts on software, often entirely from a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, layout iteration, branding concepts, and production tasks, so it can both automate portions of the work and greatly increase each designer’s output, though client interaction, taste, and higher-level creative direction still keep exposure just below the maximum.

title: Grounds maintenance workers
rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoor, and hands-on: mowing, planting, trimming, applying treatments, and operating equipment in varied real-world environments. AI may help marginally with scheduling, route planning, plant diagnostics, or autonomous equipment over time, but it does not strongly affect the core labor of maintaining landscapes.

title: Hand laborers and material movers
rationale: This work is centered on physical, hands-on tasks like lifting, moving, packing, loading, cleaning, and collecting materials in real-world environments, which limits direct AI automation. AI may improve routing, inventory tracking, warehouse coordination, and some automated handling systems, but the core job still depends heavily on human physical labor and on-site presence.

title: Hazardous materials removal workers
rationale: This job is dominated by physical, on-site work in hazardous and unpredictable environments: testing materials, building containment, operating equipment, and removing waste while following strict safety procedures. AI may help a bit with documentation, compliance support, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on cleanup and decontamination tasks.

title: Health and safety engineers
rationale: Health and safety engineers do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with, including reviewing regulations, analyzing plans and incident data, drafting reports, and creating training materials. But the job also depends heavily on site inspections, investigating real-world hazards, interviewing workers, and exercising accountable engineering judgment in physical environments, which limits full automation.

title: Health education specialists
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with core parts of this job such as creating educational materials, analyzing community data, drafting grant proposals, and evaluating programs. But much of the occupation still depends on in-person teaching, community outreach, relationship-building, empathy, and adapting to local human needs, which limits full automation.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
rationale: This job is largely digital and computer-based: organizing clinical databases, validating records, generating reports, analyzing outcomes, and supporting health information systems are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human oversight remains important because of healthcare regulations, patient privacy, data integrity, and coordination with clinical staff, but AI is likely to significantly raise productivity and reshape the role.

title: Heating, air conditioning, and refrigeration mechanics and installers
rationale: HVAC work is centered on physical installation, inspection, repair, and maintenance at customer sites, often in cramped or hazardous environments, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may help with diagnostics, scheduling, recordkeeping, and interpreting data from computerized systems, but it does not replace the hands-on mechanical work, travel, and in-person customer interaction that define the job.

title: Heavy and tractor-trailer truck drivers
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and happens in the real world, requiring on-road vehicle control, cargo inspection, securing loads, and handling unpredictable traffic and weather. That limits near-term AI automation compared with fully digital jobs, but autonomous-driving systems, route optimization, and automated logging/dispatch could still significantly reshape long-haul trucking and reduce labor demand over time.

title: Heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on inspection, disassembly, repair, and testing of large machinery in shops and field worksites, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI will help with diagnostics, manuals, service records, and troubleshooting guidance, but it is unlikely to replace the manual skill, tool use, and on-site problem solving that define the occupation.

title: High school teachers
rationale: High school teaching has meaningful AI exposure because lesson planning, content creation, grading support, parent communication, and individualized instructional materials are information-heavy tasks AI can assist with. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person classroom management, live instruction, student motivation, supervision, and relationship-building, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Historians
rationale: Historian work is heavily centered on digital and text-based tasks—researching sources, analyzing information, synthesizing findings, translating material, and writing reports or articles—all areas where AI is improving rapidly. Physical archive work, artifact handling, and nuanced interpretation of authenticity and historical significance still require human judgment, but AI is likely to substantially reshape workflows and reduce time needed for much of the core research and writing.

title: Home health and personal care aides
rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: helping clients bathe, dress, move, eat, and manage daily life in home settings. AI may assist with peripheral documentation, scheduling, or monitoring, but it cannot meaningfully replace the hands-on care, emotional support, and real-time human presence central to the occupation.

title: Human resources managers
rationale: Human resources managers do predominantly computer-based knowledge work—screening candidates, drafting policies, analyzing workforce needs, compliance, and reporting—so AI can significantly automate and accelerate large parts of the job. But the role also depends heavily on interpersonal judgment, sensitive conflict resolution, leadership, and organizational trust, which limits full automation even as AI raises productivity and may reduce staffing needs in some HR functions.

title: Human resources specialists
rationale: Human resources specialists do largely digital knowledge work—screening applicants, processing records, handling paperwork, answering policy questions, and coordinating hiring—so AI can automate or accelerate a significant share of the job. But hiring decisions, interviews, employee relations, compliance judgment, and trust-based communication still require substantial human involvement, keeping exposure high but not near-total.

title: Hydrologists
rationale: Hydrologists combine substantial computer-based work—data analysis, modeling, forecasting, and report writing—with field sampling and on-site observation that AI cannot directly replace. AI is likely to significantly boost productivity in the digital parts of the job, but physical fieldwork, scientific judgment, and collaboration with engineers and public officials keep exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Industrial designers
rationale: Industrial designers do a large share of their work digitally—researching users, generating concepts, sketching/rendering, and building CAD models—so AI can substantially accelerate ideation, visualization, and iteration. But the occupation is not fully digital: physical prototypes, manufacturing constraints, materials, product testing, and collaboration with clients and engineers still require human judgment and real-world validation.

title: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This job mixes digital analysis—studying workflows, preparing charts, interpreting drawings, and analyzing costs—with substantial on-site observation of manufacturing processes, equipment, and workers. AI can significantly assist with data analysis, scheduling, simulation, reporting, and process optimization, but the occupation still depends on physical-world context, plant-specific judgment, and collaboration on the factory floor.

title: Industrial engineers
rationale: Industrial engineers do substantial knowledge work on a computer—analyzing process data, modeling systems, designing improvements, and presenting recommendations—so AI can significantly boost productivity in core tasks. But the job also depends on observing real-world operations, understanding plant- or service-specific constraints, and coordinating implementation with people on site, which limits full automation.

title: Industrial machinery mechanics, machinery maintenance workers, and millwrights
rationale: This work is fundamentally hands-on and site-specific: inspecting, disassembling, repairing, calibrating, welding, and moving industrial machinery in factories and construction settings. AI can assist with diagnostics, predictive maintenance, manuals, and troubleshooting support, but it does not replace the core physical repair and installation tasks, and increased automation may actually increase demand for these workers.

title: Industrial production managers
rationale: This job has substantial exposure because AI can assist with production planning, data analysis, scheduling, budgeting, quality monitoring, and reporting. But exposure is limited by the need for on-site plant oversight, safety management, real-time problem solving in physical operations, and leadership of workers across a manufacturing environment.

title: Information clerks
rationale: This occupation includes a large amount of routine information processing—recordkeeping, data collection, standard responses, order handling, and basic customer inquiries—that AI and self-service software can increasingly automate or speed up. Exposure is tempered by the many roles that still require in-person service, physical presence, or handling sensitive government and customer interactions, but overall AI is likely to significantly reduce headcount and reshape workflows.

title: Information security analysts
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: monitoring systems, analyzing threats, writing reports, researching vulnerabilities, and recommending controls are all computer-based tasks where AI can already provide substantial assistance. However, cybersecurity is adversarial and high-stakes, so human judgment, real-time investigation, and accountability remain important, meaning AI is more likely to heavily augment and reshape the role than fully replace it.

title: Instructional coordinators
rationale: This job contains a large share of digital knowledge work—developing curriculum materials, analyzing test data, reviewing educational content, and recommending instructional methods—which AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But instructional coordinators also rely on in-person observation, teacher coaching, training, and relationship-based change management in schools, which limits full automation.

title: Insulation workers
rationale: Insulation work is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: measuring, cutting, fitting, spraying, climbing, and working in confined or hazardous spaces. AI may help a bit with estimating materials, reading plans, or paperwork, but it does not directly replace the core hands-on installation work.

title: Insurance sales agents
rationale: Insurance sales agents do substantial information-processing work that AI can increasingly assist or automate: lead generation, policy comparison, client analysis, quote preparation, renewals, CRM updates, and explaining standard products. But the occupation still depends heavily on trust, persuasion, relationship-building, and licensed human judgment in client interactions, which keeps exposure below the most fully digital occupations.

title: Insurance underwriters
rationale: Insurance underwriting is highly exposed because the core work is digital information processing: analyzing applications, assessing risk, and setting coverage and premiums using software. The BLS description already notes that automated underwriting tools handle many routine cases and are expected to reduce employment further, leaving mainly more complex judgment-heavy cases for humans.

title: Interior designers
