rationale: Interior design has a large digital component—creating plans, renderings, specifications, and documentation in CAD/BIM—where AI can already help with concept generation, layouts, visualization, material selection, and drafting. But the job also depends on site visits, coordination with contractors, code compliance, and nuanced client-facing aesthetic judgment, so AI is more likely to augment and compress staffing needs than fully replace the role.

title: Interpreters and translators
rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because a large share of the work—especially written translation and localization—is fully digital, and AI translation tools already automate or heavily assist core tasks through machine translation and post-editing. Exposure is somewhat lower than a 9 or 10 because live interpreting, legal/medical nuance, sign language, and high-stakes cultural judgment still require substantial human accuracy, presence, and trust.

title: Ironworkers
rationale: Ironworkers do hands-on, physical construction work in unpredictable outdoor environments, often at heights, using tools, machinery, and real-time coordination on jobsites. AI may help with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, scheduling, safety monitoring, or fabrication planning, but it does not substantially automate the core installation, alignment, cutting, and fastening work.

title: Janitors and building cleaners
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and location-specific: cleaning buildings, handling trash, restocking supplies, and making minor repairs all require in-person manual work. AI may marginally help with scheduling, supply ordering, or more advanced cleaning equipment, but it does not strongly automate the core tasks in most settings.

title: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers
rationale: This occupation is centered on hands-on physical craftsmanship: setting stones, soldering, polishing, repairing, and grading valuable items with fine manual dexterity. AI can affect design, appraisal documentation, pricing research, and digital modeling, and automation like CAD/CAM, 3D printing, and robotic polishing can raise productivity, but much of the core work still requires skilled physical manipulation and in-person handling of jewelry.

title: Judges and hearing officers
rationale: Judges and hearing officers do substantial knowledge work that AI can increasingly assist with: researching legal issues, reviewing briefs, summarizing records, and drafting opinions. But the core role also depends on human authority, procedural oversight, credibility judgments, and in-person courtroom or hearing management, which creates a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Kindergarten and elementary school teachers
rationale: AI can meaningfully help with lesson planning, worksheet creation, grading, parent communications, and adapting materials for different student needs. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person classroom management, supervising children, social-emotional development, and real-time interaction with young students, which limits full automation.

title: Labor relations specialists
rationale: This job combines substantial digital knowledge work—drafting policies, reviewing agreements, analyzing compliance, preparing materials, and documenting cases—with high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and dispute resolution. AI can meaningfully automate research, writing, summarization, and case preparation, but in-person relationship management, trust-building, and judgment in tense labor-management situations keep exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Landscape architects
rationale: Landscape architects do substantial computer-based design, drafting, visualization, estimating, and environmental analysis work, all of which AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But the job also depends on site visits, client collaboration, regulatory and environmental judgment, and coordinating real-world construction outcomes, which limits full automation.

title: Lawyers
rationale: Lawyers do a large share of their core work in fully digital domains—researching law, analyzing facts, drafting documents, and communicating advice—where AI is improving rapidly and can already boost productivity substantially. But important parts of the job still rely on human judgment, client trust, negotiation, strategy, and in-person advocacy in courts and proceedings, which keeps exposure below the maximum.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
rationale: Librarians do substantial information-search, classification, research-support, and database work that AI can increasingly assist or partially automate, especially for reference questions, discovery, and administrative tasks. But the occupation also depends on in-person patron service, teaching, community programming, collection stewardship, and institutional judgment, which creates meaningful limits on full automation.

title: Library technicians and assistants
rationale: This job mixes physical, in-person tasks like reshelving materials, staffing service desks, and running programs with routine information work such as cataloging, clerical tasks, and answering basic reference questions. AI can automate or reduce much of the digital and administrative portion, but the physical handling of materials and face-to-face patron support create a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Licensed practical and licensed vocational nurses
rationale: LPNs/LVNs do substantial hands-on, in-person care: checking vitals, changing bandages, helping patients bathe or dress, collecting samples, and responding to patient needs in real time. AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, recordkeeping, monitoring, and care coordination, but the core work depends on physical presence, human judgment, and bedside interaction.

title: Loan officers
rationale: Loan officers do substantial digital information-processing work—collecting documents, analyzing financial data, checking compliance, and making recommendations—which AI and automated underwriting can increasingly handle or accelerate. But the role also includes sales, client guidance, relationship-building, and final judgment on higher-stakes or complex loans, which keeps exposure high rather than near-total.

title: Lodging managers
rationale: Lodging managers have meaningful exposure because AI can automate or augment reservations, pricing, budgeting, forecasting, reporting, scheduling, and parts of guest communication. But the job still depends heavily on physical presence, staff supervision, inspections, and in-person problem resolution with guests, which limits full automation.

title: Logging workers
rationale: Logging work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoor, and safety-critical, involving chain saws, heavy machinery, rough terrain, and real-time coordination in unpredictable environments. AI may assist at the margins through equipment automation, log grading, or planning, but it does not substantially replace the core hands-on work of felling, moving, and handling timber.

title: Logisticians
rationale: Logisticians do predominantly digital planning, forecasting, analysis, scheduling, and coordination work, all of which are increasingly supportable or partially automatable with AI. But the occupation is tied to real-world supply chains, supplier relationships, and exception handling in fast-moving physical operations, which keeps human judgment and cross-organizational coordination important.

title: Machinists and tool and die makers
rationale: This occupation is centered on physical, hands-on manufacturing work: setting up machines, handling materials, measuring tolerances, and operating in a shop environment, which limits direct AI automation. AI and automation can meaningfully affect the digital portions—CAD/CAM, CNC programming, process optimization, and inspection—and the BLS already notes technology-driven efficiency gains reducing demand for some tasks, but the core job still requires in-person mechanical skill and precision.

title: Management analysts
rationale: Management analysts do predominantly digital knowledge work: gathering information, analyzing data, developing recommendations, and producing reports and presentations are all tasks where AI can significantly assist or partially automate. However, client interviews, onsite observation, relationship management, and persuading leaders to adopt changes still require substantial human judgment and interpersonal skill, keeping exposure below the maximum.

title: Manicurists and pedicurists
rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: cleaning, shaping, polishing, and decorating nails with manual dexterity and direct client contact. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, design ideas, or inventory, but it does little to automate the main service itself.

title: Marine engineers and naval architects
rationale: This is largely engineering knowledge work: designing vessels and systems, producing drawings and schematics, analyzing performance, estimating costs, and writing technical reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with. But the occupation also depends on physical-world constraints, safety-critical judgment, regulatory compliance, and on-site testing/oversight in shipyards, coastal sites, or at sea, which limit full automation.

title: Market research analysts
rationale: This job is predominantly digital knowledge work: collecting data, analyzing trends, designing surveys, interpreting results, and producing reports and presentations. AI can already automate or greatly accelerate much of the research, statistical analysis, synthesis, and writing, though human judgment is still important for framing questions, validating insights, and advising clients.

title: Marriage and family therapists
rationale: Marriage and family therapists do substantial information-processing work—assessment, treatment planning, documentation, and coordination—where AI can assist and improve productivity. But the core job depends on high-trust human interaction, empathy, nuanced judgment, and live therapeutic relationships, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Masonry workers
rationale: Masonry work is overwhelmingly physical, site-based, and hands-on: lifting materials, laying brick or stone, finishing concrete, and adjusting to real-world conditions on construction sites. AI may help with peripheral tasks like estimating materials, reading plans, or scheduling, but it does little to automate the core manual craft, so overall exposure is low.

title: Massage therapists
rationale: Massage therapy’s core work is highly physical, hands-on, and requires in-person human presence, touch, and real-time assessment of clients’ bodies. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, documentation, or treatment planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the central service itself.

title: Material moving machine operators
rationale: This job is centered on physically operating machinery in real-world environments like warehouses, ports, and construction sites, which limits direct AI substitution compared with digital desk work. However, AI-enabled automation, sensors, scanners, and autonomous material-handling systems can increasingly take over parts of the core task and reduce labor demand, especially in structured settings such as warehouses.

title: Material recording clerks
rationale: This job combines routine digital recordkeeping, scheduling, inventory tracking, and report compilation—tasks that AI and adjacent automation can increasingly handle or streamline. But many clerks also need physical presence in warehouses or plants to inspect goods, scan items, handle packages, and coordinate real-world material flow, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Materials engineers
rationale: Materials engineers do substantial knowledge work on computers—simulation, analysis, coding, reporting, and material selection—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity and assist with design, prediction, and documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on physical testing, lab and factory work, real-world validation, and engineering judgment tied to manufacturing constraints, which limits full automation.

title: Mathematicians and statisticians
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: defining data needs, designing studies, building models, analyzing results, writing code, and communicating findings are all tasks AI is rapidly improving at. Human judgment, methodological rigor, and domain expertise still matter, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and increase output per worker.

title: Mechanical engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: This occupation has substantial exposure because important parts of the work are digital and information-based: reviewing drawings, using 3D design software, analyzing test data, making calculations, and documenting recommendations. But exposure is capped by the hands-on physical side of the job—assembling parts, operating machinery, setting up tests, troubleshooting equipment, and working in factories or labs—which still requires human presence and manual skill.

title: Mechanical engineers
rationale: Mechanical engineers do substantial digital knowledge work—CAD design, simulation, analysis, troubleshooting, and documentation—where AI can already boost productivity and may automate meaningful portions over time. But the occupation also depends on physical-world testing, prototyping, manufacturing oversight, and engineering judgment about real systems, which limits full automation.

title: Medical and health services managers
rationale: This job includes substantial digital knowledge work—budgeting, reporting, scheduling, compliance monitoring, record management, and analyzing healthcare metrics—where AI can significantly boost productivity. But the role also depends heavily on human leadership, staff supervision, cross-functional coordination, and on-site operational judgment in real healthcare settings, which limits full automation.

title: Medical assistants
rationale: Medical assistants have meaningful exposure because a substantial share of their administrative work—scheduling, documentation, insurance forms, coding, and record entry—is digital and can be assisted or partly automated by AI. But the occupation also includes many core in-person clinical tasks like taking vital signs, drawing blood, giving injections, preparing specimens, and assisting with exams, which require physical presence and keep overall exposure moderate.

title: Medical dosimetrists
rationale: Medical dosimetrists do highly specialized computer-based work: reviewing scans, calculating radiation doses, and designing treatment plans, all of which are strong targets for AI assistance and partial automation. But the job remains constrained by clinical oversight, safety-critical judgment, regulatory requirements, equipment QA, and coordination with the oncology team, so AI is more likely to augment and raise productivity than fully replace the role soon.

title: Medical equipment repairers
rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on: installing, diagnosing, calibrating, and physically repairing complex medical equipment on site in hospitals and clinics. AI can help with manuals, troubleshooting support, documentation, software updates, and diagnostics, but it does not replace the need for physical presence, tool use, regulatory checks, and real-world repair work.

title: Medical records specialists
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, centered on reviewing documentation, entering data, assigning standardized codes, and managing records in EHR systems—all tasks in domains where AI is advancing rapidly. Human oversight, compliance, and occasional clarification with clinicians still matter, but the core work is highly susceptible to automation and major productivity gains from AI.

title: Medical scientists
rationale: Medical scientists do substantial computer-based work—analyzing data, reviewing literature, designing studies, writing grants, and publishing findings—so AI can significantly boost productivity in important parts of the job. But core research still depends heavily on physical lab work, clinical trials, handling biological samples, and high-stakes scientific judgment, which limits full automation.

title: Medical transcriptionists
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, consisting of converting, editing, and entering dictated medical information into standardized records on a computer. AI speech recognition and natural language processing already perform much of the core work today, with humans increasingly limited to review and error correction, which is why the occupation is already declining.

title: Meeting, convention, and event planners
rationale: AI can assist significantly with the digital parts of this job—drafting communications, comparing venues and vendors, building schedules and budgets, and analyzing attendee feedback. But the occupation still depends heavily on in-person site visits, live event coordination, vendor/client relationship management, and real-time problem solving in physical settings, which limits full automation.

title: Metal and plastic machine workers
rationale: This work is centered on physically setting up, loading, monitoring, and maintaining industrial machines on a factory floor, which limits direct substitution by AI alone. However, the occupation is meaningfully exposed through automation, CNC programming, robotics, and AI-enabled process control that let fewer workers oversee more machines and reduce demand for many routine operator roles.

title: Microbiologists
rationale: Microbiologists do meaningful computer-based work—analyzing data, writing reports, reviewing literature, and preparing grant applications—so AI can significantly assist with the information-processing parts of the job. But the core occupation still depends heavily on physical laboratory experiments, specimen handling, specialized equipment, and scientific judgment in real-world research settings, which limits full automation.

title: Middle school teachers
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, creating materials, grading some assignments, test preparation, and parent communications, which raises productivity in the knowledge-work parts of teaching. But the core of the job still depends on in-person classroom management, motivating adolescents, adapting in real time to student behavior and needs, and providing human supervision, so AI is more likely to augment than replace most middle school teachers.

title: Military careers
rationale: Military careers span many specialties, but the occupation overall is grounded in physical presence, operational readiness, leadership, and work in dangerous real-world environments that AI cannot replace directly. AI will increasingly affect planning, intelligence, logistics, administration, and some technical roles, but much of the core work still requires human judgment, command responsibility, teamwork, and on-site action.

title: Mining and geological engineers
rationale: Mining and geological engineers do substantial analytical and design work on computers—modeling mines, evaluating sites, monitoring production, and writing technical reports—so AI can assist meaningfully with planning, analysis, and documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on site-specific physical conditions, field inspections, safety oversight, and coordination with operations in real mining environments, which limits full automation.

title: Models
rationale: Modeling depends heavily on physical appearance, in-person photo shoots, runway shows, and live brand representation, which limits full automation. But AI-generated imagery and image reuse can directly reduce demand for human models in digital advertising and other visual media, creating meaningful indirect exposure.

title: Music directors and composers
rationale: AI is increasingly capable of generating, arranging, and editing music, so the composing side of this occupation faces meaningful exposure, especially for routine commercial or background music work done digitally. But a large share of the job—conducting ensembles, directing rehearsals, giving artistic feedback, auditioning performers, and leading live performances—depends on in-person leadership, musical interpretation, and real-time human coordination.

title: Musicians and singers
rationale: This job’s core value is embodied live performance, rehearsing, touring, and audience connection, which limits direct automation by AI. But AI is increasingly relevant for recorded music production, background/session work, promotion, and even generating music itself, so parts of the occupation—especially its digital and commercial side—face meaningful disruption.

title: Natural sciences managers
rationale: Natural sciences managers do substantial digital knowledge work—planning, budgeting, reviewing research, reporting, and communicating—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in many core tasks. But the occupation also depends on leadership, scientific judgment, cross-functional coordination, hiring, and oversight of labs or fieldwork, which keeps exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Network and computer systems administrators
rationale: This is predominantly digital work—configuring systems, monitoring performance, managing permissions, troubleshooting, and maintaining security—so AI can increasingly automate or accelerate a large share of the core tasks. Exposure is not maximal because the job still involves responsibility for real-world infrastructure, outage response, environment-specific judgment, and occasional hands-on hardware/network work, but AI and automation are likely to significantly reduce labor needed per system administered.

title: News analysts, reporters, and journalists
rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because much of its core work is digital information processing: researching, analyzing, writing, editing, summarizing, and producing content for multiple platforms. AI can already assist substantially with drafting, background research, transcription, headline generation, and story adaptation, though in-person reporting, source development, interviews, and on-the-ground judgment keep it from being near the maximum.

title: Nuclear engineers
rationale: Nuclear engineering is heavily knowledge-based and computer-centered: modeling, analysis, documentation, compliance review, and technical design are all areas where AI can substantially assist and boost productivity. But the occupation also involves high-stakes safety judgment, regulatory accountability, experiments, and oversight of physical facilities, which creates an important barrier to full automation.

title: Nuclear medicine technologists
rationale: This job has some digital and technical components—operating imaging equipment, keeping records, and assessing procedures—where AI can assist with workflow, documentation, and decision support. But the core work requires in-person patient care, administering radiopharmaceuticals, operating specialized equipment, and following strict physical safety protocols, which creates a substantial barrier to automation.

title: Nuclear technicians
rationale: Nuclear technicians do some digital monitoring, calculations, and documentation that AI can assist with, especially in anomaly detection, analysis, and reporting. But the core job also requires on-site equipment operation, physical inspections, radiation measurement, safety compliance, and incident response in tightly regulated environments, which limits full automation.

title: Nurse anesthetists, nurse midwives, and nurse practitioners
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist APRNs with documentation, test interpretation, decision support, and care planning, so the information-processing parts of the job are moderately exposed. But the core work still depends heavily on physical exams, procedures, bedside monitoring, patient counseling, and real-time clinical judgment in high-stakes settings, which limits full automation.

title: Nursing assistants and orderlies
rationale: This work is centered on hands-on physical care: bathing, feeding, lifting, repositioning, transporting patients, cleaning, and responding to people in person. AI may help a bit with documentation, monitoring, or scheduling, but it does not meaningfully automate the core bedside and facility-based tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Occupational health and safety specialists and technicians
rationale: AI can help meaningfully with the desk-based parts of this job—analyzing incident data, interpreting regulations, generating reports, and creating training materials. But the core role still depends heavily on in-person inspections, fieldwork in hazardous environments, observing worksites directly, and enforcing safety practices on site, which limits full automation.

title: Occupational therapists
rationale: Occupational therapists’ core work is hands-on, in-person care: evaluating patients, demonstrating exercises, adapting environments, and building rapport with clients and families. AI can assist with documentation, treatment planning, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the physical presence, clinical observation, and interpersonal coaching central to the job.

title: Occupational therapy assistants and aides
rationale: Most core duties are hands-on, in-person patient care: guiding therapeutic activities, moving clients, setting up equipment, and observing progress in real time. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, billing forms, and some treatment-planning support, but it does not replace the physical assistance, interpersonal coaching, and clinical presence central to the job.

title: Oil and gas workers
rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, site-based, and equipment-focused, often in hazardous outdoor or offshore environments where human presence, manual skill, and crew coordination are essential. AI may reduce some labor indirectly through automated drilling, robotics, and remote monitoring, but it does not strongly automate the core hands-on tasks of operating, moving, repairing, and maintaining oilfield equipment.

title: Operations research analysts
rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: collecting and analyzing data, building quantitative models, using analytical software, and writing reports and recommendations. AI is rapidly improving in exactly these domains, so it can automate substantial portions of the work and greatly increase each analyst’s productivity, though human judgment and stakeholder persuasion still matter.

title: Opticians
rationale: Opticians’ core work is hands-on and customer-facing: measuring faces, fitting and adjusting eyewear, teaching contact lens use, and helping people choose products in person. AI may help with peripheral tasks like inventory, records, sales support, and online frame recommendations, but it does not strongly automate the physical fitting and service components.

title: Optometrists
rationale: Optometrists’ core work depends on in-person eye exams, use of specialized equipment, clinical judgment, and direct patient care, which limits full automation. AI can meaningfully assist with image analysis, screening, documentation, and prescription support, but it is more likely to augment productivity than replace the occupation’s hands-on clinical role.

title: Orthotists and prosthetists
rationale: This job has some AI exposure in design, measurement analysis, documentation, and possibly computer-aided customization of devices. But the core work remains heavily physical and patient-facing: evaluating bodies in person, fitting and adjusting devices, using hands-on fabrication tools, and working directly with patients and care teams.

title: Painters, construction and maintenance
rationale: This job’s core tasks are physical and on-site: preparing surfaces, climbing ladders, handling tools, and applying coatings in real-world environments. AI may help with peripheral work like estimating materials, scheduling, or color selection, but it does little to automate the hands-on painting itself.

title: Painting and coating workers
rationale: This job is centered on physical, in-person work: preparing surfaces, operating spray/coating equipment, monitoring finishes, and doing manual touch-ups in factory or repair settings. AI may modestly increase automation through smarter machine control, inspection, and process optimization, but the core work still depends heavily on hands-on operation in the physical world.

title: Paralegals and legal assistants
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital and document-based: legal research, drafting, organizing files, reviewing transcripts, and coordinating case materials are all core tasks where AI is rapidly improving. Some in-person trial support and client interaction remain, but AI can substantially automate or accelerate much of the occupation’s main work, likely reducing labor demand per case.

title: Personal financial advisors
rationale: Personal financial advisors do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing portfolios, researching investments, modeling scenarios, and communicating recommendations—so AI can automate or greatly accelerate much of the core information-processing work. But the occupation also depends heavily on trust, relationship-building, sales, and nuanced client judgment around life goals and risk tolerance, which should keep humans important even as AI raises productivity and reshapes the role.

title: Pest control workers
rationale: This job is dominated by in-person physical work: inspecting buildings, crawling into tight spaces, applying treatments, setting traps, driving to sites, and interacting with customers on location. AI may help with scheduling, recordkeeping, pricing, or pest identification, but it is unlikely to automate the core hands-on fieldwork.

title: Petroleum engineers
rationale: Petroleum engineering includes substantial computer-based analysis, modeling, design, and technical planning, so AI can meaningfully assist with reservoir analysis, drilling plans, optimization, and documentation. But the job is still tightly tied to physical operations, field conditions, safety, equipment, and site coordination, which limits full automation and keeps human engineering judgment and on-site oversight central.

title: Pharmacists
rationale: Pharmacists have meaningful exposure because medication review, drug interaction checks, documentation, insurance coordination, and patient information tasks are information-heavy and increasingly AI-assisted. But the occupation still depends heavily on licensed human judgment, legal accountability, patient counseling, supervision, and in-person clinical/retail work such as dispensing and vaccinations, which limits full automation.

title: Pharmacy technicians
rationale: Pharmacy technicians have meaningful exposure because parts of the job involve routine information processing: entering patient data, processing insurance claims, managing inventory, and handling basic customer communications. But core work still requires physical presence, medication handling, packaging, hospital/retail workflow, and close supervision in a regulated safety-critical environment, which limits full AI replacement.

title: Phlebotomists
rationale: Phlebotomy’s core tasks—drawing blood, handling specimens, maintaining sterile equipment, and calming patients in person—require physical dexterity and real-world human interaction, which limits direct AI automation. AI may help with peripheral work like data entry, labeling checks, scheduling, and documentation, but it does not substantially replace the hands-on clinical role.

title: Photographers
rationale: Photography has meaningful AI exposure because image editing, enhancement, archiving, marketing, and some commercial image creation are digital tasks that AI can increasingly automate or compress. But the occupation still relies heavily on physical presence, equipment handling, real-world image capture, and client interaction—especially for weddings, portraits, events, and on-location shoots—so AI is more likely to reshape parts of the job than fully replace it.

title: Physical therapist assistants and aides
rationale: Most core tasks are hands-on and in-person: guiding exercises, moving patients, using equipment, cleaning treatment areas, and observing physical progress. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, insurance forms, and treatment planning support, but it is unlikely to replace the physical, interpersonal, and safety-critical parts of the job.

title: Physical therapists
rationale: Physical therapists’ core work is hands-on, in-person, and highly physical: observing movement, manipulating patients, guiding exercises, and building trust during treatment. AI can assist with documentation, treatment planning, and reviewing records, but it is unlikely to replace the real-time physical care and patient interaction at the center of the job.

title: Physician assistants
rationale: Physician assistants do significant in-person clinical work—examining patients, performing procedures, prescribing, and coordinating care—which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. But AI can meaningfully reshape the job by assisting with documentation, history-taking, diagnostic support, test interpretation, and treatment planning, increasing productivity in the information-processing parts of care.

title: Physicians and surgeons
rationale: AI will significantly assist physicians with documentation, image and test interpretation, decision support, and treatment planning, especially in more information-heavy specialties like radiology and pathology. But the occupation overall still depends heavily on in-person exams, procedures and surgery, real-time judgment under uncertainty, patient trust, and legal accountability, which limit full automation.

title: Physicists and astronomers
rationale: Physicists and astronomers do a large share of their work in highly digital domains—mathematical modeling, coding, data analysis, paper writing, and grant writing—where AI can already provide substantial assistance and boost productivity. But the occupation also depends on original scientific judgment, experimental design, collaboration, and in many cases specialized physical equipment or observations, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Plumbers, pipefitters, and steamfitters
rationale: This job’s core tasks are physical, on-site, and manual: installing, repairing, testing, and troubleshooting piping systems in homes, buildings, and industrial settings. AI may help with peripheral work like estimates, blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or BIM-assisted planning, but it does not substantially automate the hands-on trade work that defines the occupation.

title: Podiatrists
rationale: Podiatrists do substantial knowledge work in diagnosis, imaging review, documentation, and treatment planning, all of which AI can increasingly assist. But the core of the occupation still depends heavily on in-person physical exams, procedures, surgery, and direct patient care, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Police and detectives
rationale: Police and detective work is centered on physical presence, field response, patrol, arrests, evidence collection, and real-time judgment in unpredictable situations, which creates a strong barrier to direct AI automation. AI can still meaningfully affect paperwork, report writing, record searches, case preparation, and some investigative analysis, but it is unlikely to replace the core in-person enforcement role.

title: Political scientists
rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: researching, analyzing survey and policy data, forecasting trends, and writing/presenting findings are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human judgment, domain expertise, and credibility still matter for interpreting complex political contexts and shaping research agendas, but the occupation is highly exposed because much of the core workflow happens on a computer.

title: Postal service workers
rationale: Postal service work is centered on physical mail handling, delivery routes, customer service, and operating equipment, which limits direct AI substitution. However, AI and automation can significantly reduce labor in mail sorting, address recognition, route planning, and some counter-service tasks, so the occupation faces moderate indirect exposure even though the core work is not fully digital.

title: Postsecondary education administrators
rationale: This is largely knowledge and administrative work—reviewing applications, analyzing student data, managing records, preparing communications, scheduling, and handling routine advising—all areas where AI can significantly assist or partially automate tasks. But the role also depends on human judgment, institutional accountability, sensitive student and faculty interactions, and on-campus coordination, which limits full replacement.

title: Postsecondary teachers
rationale: Postsecondary teaching includes a large share of digital knowledge work—creating course materials, grading, writing, research, and communication—where AI can already boost productivity substantially. But the occupation still depends heavily on live instruction, mentoring, classroom presence, and subject-matter judgment, so AI is more likely to augment and restructure faculty work than fully replace it.

title: Power plant operators, distributors, and dispatchers
rationale: AI and automation can meaningfully reduce headcount in this occupation by improving monitoring, control-room decision support, and automating some dispatch and rerouting tasks; the BLS already notes smart-grid technology and modernized control rooms as reducing employment. But the work remains tied to safety-critical physical infrastructure, on-site operations, emergency response, and licensed human oversight, which limits full automation of the core job.

title: Preschool and childcare center directors
rationale: AI can assist with substantial administrative parts of the job—budgeting, scheduling, drafting communications, recordkeeping, and some program planning—making directors more efficient. But the role’s core still depends heavily on onsite leadership, supervising staff, interacting with parents and children, and ensuring facility and regulatory compliance in a physical setting.

title: Preschool teachers
rationale: AI can help with peripheral knowledge-work tasks like lesson planning, activity ideas, documentation, and parent communications. But the core of the job is in-person care, supervision, play-based teaching, and emotional interaction with very young children, which strongly limits automation and large labor reduction.

title: Private detectives and investigators
rationale: This job combines digital information work—searching records, analyzing evidence, and writing reports—with physical surveillance, fieldwork, and in-person interviewing. AI can significantly boost the research, pattern-finding, and documentation parts of investigations, but the core work still often depends on lawful real-world observation, human judgment, and physical presence.

title: Probation officers and correctional treatment specialists
rationale: This job combines significant paperwork, reporting, and risk assessment—areas where AI can help with documentation, case summaries, and decision support—with core duties that require in-person supervision, interviews, home visits, court participation, and relationship management. AI will likely raise productivity on the administrative side, but the occupation’s legal accountability, fieldwork, and human judgment needs limit full automation.

title: Producers and directors
rationale: Producers and directors are significantly exposed because many core tasks—developing concepts, evaluating scripts, planning productions, overseeing editing/postproduction, and promotion—are information-rich and increasingly supported by AI tools. But the job also depends heavily on in-person leadership, creative taste, fundraising, and directing actors and crews in physical production environments, which limits full automation.

title: Project management specialists
rationale: Project management specialists do predominantly digital knowledge work—planning, scheduling, budgeting, tracking milestones, producing documents, and coordinating information—all areas where AI can substantially assist or automate parts of the workflow. But the role also depends heavily on human judgment, stakeholder management, negotiation, and accountability across teams, which makes full replacement harder even as AI boosts each worker’s productivity.

title: Property appraisers and assessors
rationale: This job combines significant computer-based analysis—reviewing records, running comparables, maintaining databases, and writing valuation reports—with physical site visits and local judgment about property condition and context. AI and automated valuation models can substantially speed the analytical and documentation side, especially for assessors using mass appraisal systems, but in-person inspection, defending assessments, and nuanced market judgment keep exposure below fully digital occupations.

title: Property, real estate, and community association managers
rationale: This job mixes sizable administrative and digital work—leasing communications, recordkeeping, budgeting, reporting, marketing vacancies, and maintenance coordination—that AI can increasingly automate or streamline. But much of the core role still depends on physical presence, property inspections, showing units, contractor oversight, and handling tenant or homeowner disputes, which limits full automation.

title: Psychiatric technicians and aides
rationale: This job’s core work is in-person patient care: monitoring behavior, helping with daily activities, escorting patients, administering treatments, and sometimes physically restraining people. AI may assist with documentation, intake, risk flagging, or care planning, but it does not replace the hands-on, real-time human presence and judgment central to the role.

title: Psychologists
rationale: Psychologists have meaningful exposure because AI can assist with testing, documentation, report writing, research, and some aspects of assessment or treatment planning. But much of the occupation depends on trust, nuanced observation, ethical judgment, licensure, and direct human interaction with clients, students, families, or organizations, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Public relations and fundraising managers
rationale: This job has high AI exposure because much of the work is digital information and communication work: drafting press releases, speeches, campaigns, grant materials, audience analysis, and monitoring media trends. However, it is not near-maximum exposure because core value still depends on human judgment, crisis management, leadership, and relationship-building with executives, media, and especially donors in face-to-face settings.

title: Public relations specialists
rationale: This job is heavily centered on digital communication tasks—writing press releases, drafting speeches, monitoring social media, shaping messaging, and responding to media inquiries—all areas where AI can already generate, summarize, analyze, and optimize output. Human judgment, relationship-building with clients and media, and crisis communication still matter, but AI is likely to significantly increase productivity and reduce the amount of routine content and monitoring work needing human labor.

title: Public safety telecommunicators
rationale: This job is heavily information-processing and computer-mediated, so AI can assist with call intake, transcription, protocol guidance, translation, routing, recordkeeping, and some triage. But the core work is real-time, high-stakes emergency judgment and emotionally sensitive communication with callers and responders, where reliability, accountability, and human oversight remain critical.

title: Purchasing managers, buyers, and purchasing agents
rationale: This is largely office-based knowledge work involving analyzing proposals, comparing suppliers, reviewing contracts, maintaining records, and communicating with vendors—all areas where AI can substantially boost productivity. But the job also depends on negotiation, supplier relationships, strategic judgment, and sometimes site visits or product evaluation, which keep it from the very highest exposure tier.

title: Quality control inspectors
rationale: This job’s core work happens in the physical world: inspecting materials, measuring parts, monitoring production lines, and handling products on-site. AI and machine vision can automate parts of defect detection, reporting, and process monitoring, but human inspectors still need physical presence, judgment, and hands-on verification in manufacturing environments.

title: Radiation therapists
rationale: Radiation therapists’ core work requires physical presence: positioning patients, operating radiation equipment, monitoring reactions, and maintaining strict safety procedures in clinical settings. AI may increasingly assist with treatment planning, imaging, documentation, and workflow efficiency, but it is unlikely to replace the hands-on patient care and in-person machine operation that define the job.

title: Radiologic and MRI technologists
rationale: AI will significantly affect parts of this job such as image quality optimization, scan protocol guidance, workflow, documentation, and preliminary image assessment. But the core work still requires in-person patient positioning, equipment operation, safety procedures, contrast administration, and hands-on interaction in clinical settings, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Railroad workers
rationale: Railroad work is rooted in physical operations, on-site safety monitoring, equipment handling, and real-time coordination in trains and rail yards, which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. However, dispatching, scheduling, diagnostics, signaling, and some train operation tasks are already being automated, so AI will likely reduce staffing needs and reshape parts of the occupation over time.

title: Real estate brokers and sales agents
rationale: AI can significantly assist with the occupation’s information-heavy tasks: generating listings and ads, analyzing comparable properties, drafting documents, answering client questions, and automating lead generation and follow-up. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person property showings, local relationship-building, trust, and live negotiation, which limits full automation even as AI may reduce the number of agents needed per transaction.

title: Receptionists
rationale: Receptionist work includes many routine information-processing tasks that AI and software can increasingly handle: answering common questions, routing calls, scheduling appointments, data entry, and managing correspondence. However, the job still often requires physical presence at a front desk, greeting and checking in visitors, and handling in-person interactions and security-related tasks, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Recreation workers
rationale: This job’s core work is in-person: leading activities, supervising groups, adapting to participants’ needs, ensuring safety, and providing basic first aid. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, paperwork, newsletters, and activity planning, but it does not replace the physical presence, real-time interaction, and hands-on supervision central to the role.

title: Recreational therapists
rationale: Recreational therapists do meaningful documentation, assessment, and treatment-planning work that AI can assist with, especially around records review, progress notes, and program ideas. But the core of the job is highly in-person and physical: observing patients directly, building trust, leading therapeutic activities, and adapting in real time to patients’ emotional and physical needs.

title: Registered nurses
rationale: Registered nurses have meaningful exposure because AI can assist with documentation, patient monitoring, care planning, diagnostic support, and analyzing test results, making the information-heavy parts of the job more efficient. But the core occupation still depends heavily on bedside physical care, real-time judgment in clinical settings, and human interaction with patients and families, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Rehabilitation counselors
rationale: Rehabilitation counselors combine significant documentation, assessment, care planning, and resource-matching work that AI can increasingly assist with. But the core of the occupation depends on trust, empathy, counseling, advocacy, and coordination with clients and families in sensitive real-world situations, which limits full automation.

title: Respiratory therapists
rationale: Respiratory therapists do hands-on clinical work: examining patients, administering treatments, operating ventilators, collecting samples, and responding in person in hospitals or homes. AI may help with monitoring, documentation, and treatment decision support, but the core job depends on physical presence, patient interaction, and real-time clinical judgment.

title: Retail sales workers
rationale: Retail sales work depends heavily on physical presence in stores, face-to-face customer service, stocking, and handling merchandise, which limits direct automation by AI. However, AI and related technologies can reduce labor needs indirectly through better online shopping, automated checkout, inventory systems, and product recommendation tools, so the occupation faces meaningful but not extreme exposure.

title: Roofers
rationale: Roofing is overwhelmingly physical, hands-on outdoor work that requires climbing, lifting, measuring, cutting, and installing materials on real buildings. AI may help a little with estimates, scheduling, or paperwork, but it does not meaningfully automate the core task of repairing and installing roofs.

title: Sales engineers
rationale: Sales engineers do substantial knowledge work on a computer—preparing presentations, analyzing customer requirements, tailoring solutions, and providing technical support—so AI can meaningfully automate or accelerate large parts of the job. But core value still depends heavily on relationship-building, persuasion, negotiation, and customer-facing interactions, often with travel and live technical discussions, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Sales managers
rationale: Sales managers do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing sales data, forecasting, budgeting, setting goals, and coordinating strategy—where AI can significantly boost productivity and automate parts of planning and reporting. But the job also depends heavily on leadership, hiring, coaching, negotiation, relationship management, and handling escalated customer issues, which keep human judgment and interpersonal presence central.

title: School and career counselors and advisors
rationale: AI can assist with assessments, recordkeeping, résumé and application guidance, labor-market information, and identifying at-risk students from data, so the information-processing parts of the job are meaningfully exposed. But the core work still depends heavily on trust, empathy, sensitive judgment, and live interaction with students, families, and staff in school or counseling settings, which limits full automation.

title: Secretaries and administrative assistants
rationale: This occupation is heavily exposed because much of the core work is routine digital information processing: scheduling, document preparation, email and phone handling, database maintenance, transcription, and basic bookkeeping. AI can automate or greatly accelerate many of these tasks, and even where full replacement is limited by coordination or interpersonal needs, each worker can support more people, reducing headcount demand.

title: Securities, commodities, and financial services sales agents
rationale: This job is heavily exposed because much of its core work is digital information processing: analyzing markets, evaluating deals, monitoring securities, and executing trades, all areas where AI and automation are advancing quickly. However, the occupation still depends substantially on client relationships, persuasion, trust, regulatory accountability, and high-stakes judgment, which limits full automation even as AI reduces demand for some roles like routine traders and brokers.

title: Security guards and gambling surveillance officers
rationale: This work is centered on physical presence, patrolling property, controlling access, and responding to incidents in real time, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI replacement. However, AI-enabled cameras, remote monitoring, and automated alerting can reduce demand for some monitoring and surveillance tasks, especially for gambling surveillance officers and stationary guard roles.

title: Semiconductor processing technicians
rationale: This job is centered on physical, on-site manufacturing work in cleanrooms: operating equipment, handling wafers, adjusting machinery, and inspecting output. AI can meaningfully improve process monitoring, defect detection, documentation, and optimization, but the core role still depends on human presence around specialized equipment and real-world production systems.

title: Set and exhibit designers
rationale: Set and exhibit designers do substantial digital knowledge work—concept development, visual design, CAD drafting, and budget/planning—where AI can already assist and may significantly boost productivity. But the job also depends heavily on site-specific physical constraints, collaboration with directors/curators, and overseeing real-world installation, which limits full automation.

title: Sheet metal workers
rationale: Sheet metal work is dominated by physical fabrication, installation, and repair in shops and construction sites, requiring manual skill, strength, and on-site presence. AI may modestly affect planning, CADD use, and automated fabrication equipment, but it does not directly replace the core hands-on tasks for most workers.

title: Skincare specialists
rationale: Skincare specialists do hands-on, in-person physical work: evaluating skin, performing treatments, hair removal, massages, and maintaining sanitary equipment and spaces. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, product recommendations, or basic skin-analysis tools, but it does not replace the core client-facing physical services.

title: Small engine mechanics
rationale: Small engine mechanics do predominantly hands-on physical repair work that requires in-person inspection, disassembly, tool use, and testing of real equipment in shops, marinas, or onsite. AI can help with diagnostics, service documentation, and accessing repair information, but it is unlikely to automate the core manual troubleshooting and repair tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Social and community service managers
rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge-work that AI can assist with—data analysis, reporting, proposal writing, budgeting, planning, and administrative oversight. But core effectiveness still depends heavily on human judgment, staff supervision, community relationships, fundraising, and in-person coordination, so AI is more likely to augment than fully replace the role.

title: Social and human service assistants
rationale: This job mixes paperwork, benefits research, service coordination, and follow-up—tasks AI can increasingly assist with—with substantial in-person, relationship-based work with vulnerable clients. Because the core role depends on trust, empathy, judgment, and navigating local real-world services, AI is more likely to augment productivity than fully replace the occupation.

title: Social workers
rationale: Social workers have meaningful exposure because AI can help with documentation, case management, resource referral, treatment planning support, and other administrative information-processing tasks. But the core of the job relies on in-person trust, empathy, crisis response, advocacy, and licensed clinical judgment in complex human situations, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Sociologists
rationale: Sociologists do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing studies, analyzing data, synthesizing findings, and writing reports and presentations, all of which are areas where AI is advancing quickly. Human judgment remains important for research design, interpreting social context, interviewing people, and policy relevance, but AI can substantially increase productivity and reshape much of the occupation.

title: Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: designing software, writing or specifying code, testing, documenting, and communicating about requirements are all tasks AI can increasingly perform or accelerate. Human judgment, system design, stakeholder coordination, and accountability still matter, but AI is likely to substantially boost productivity and reshape team structure, workflows, and staffing needs.

title: Solar photovoltaic installers
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: measuring roofs, mounting panels, using tools, climbing ladders, wiring systems, and performing outdoor maintenance. AI may help with peripheral tasks like system design, planning, diagnostics, or paperwork, but the core work requires hands-on installation and real-world safety in unpredictable environments.

title: Special education teachers
rationale: Special education teaching includes substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with, such as drafting IEPs, adapting lessons, preparing materials, tracking progress, and handling documentation. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person instruction, behavioral support, individualized judgment, and close human relationships with students, parents, and staff, which limits full automation.

title: Special effects artists and animators
rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital: creating graphics, animation, visual effects, and sometimes code on a computer, often even from home. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, style transfer, rotoscoping, motion, and other core production tasks, so it is likely to significantly reshape workflows and reduce labor needed for many routine or iterative parts, even though human creativity, taste, and collaboration still matter.

title: Speech-language pathologists
rationale: Speech-language pathologists do substantial assessment, documentation, and treatment planning that AI can assist with, and AI tools may increasingly support screening, progress notes, and therapy materials. But the core of the job depends on in-person clinical observation, hands-on swallowing treatment, and sustained human interaction with patients and families, which limits full automation.

title: Stationary engineers and boiler operators
rationale: This job is centered on operating, inspecting, repairing, and maintaining physical boiler and building systems in person, often in hazardous industrial environments. AI may help with monitoring, diagnostics, predictive maintenance, and paperwork, but the core work still depends on hands-on mechanical skill, on-site judgment, and continuous physical presence.

title: Substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors
rationale: This job combines significant knowledge work with deeply human, trust-based interaction: counseling, crisis intervention, assessment, and relationship-building are central and hard to automate fully. AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, intake, treatment-plan drafting, psychoeducation, and some lower-acuity support, but the core therapeutic role still depends on licensed human judgment and interpersonal presence.

title: Surgical assistants and technologists
rationale: This work is centered on physical, in-person assistance in operating rooms: preparing sterile fields, handling instruments, positioning patients, and directly assisting surgeons during live procedures. AI may help with peripheral tasks such as documentation, supply tracking, or robotic support, but it does not meaningfully replace the core hands-on, real-time surgical role.

title: Survey researchers
rationale: Survey researchers do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing questionnaires, analyzing data with statistical software, troubleshooting sampling issues, and presenting findings. AI can already assist substantially with survey drafting, data analysis, visualization, and reporting, and newer data-mining tools may reduce demand for traditional survey work, though human judgment still matters for methodology, validity, and interpretation.

title: Surveying and mapping technicians
rationale: This occupation has a meaningful digital component—processing survey data, updating GIS databases, using CAD, and working with aerial imagery—where AI can assist with image analysis, map updates, calculations, and documentation. But a large share of the job still requires physical fieldwork, operating instruments, traveling to sites, and collecting measurements in real-world conditions, which limits full automation.

title: Surveyors
rationale: Surveyors have meaningful exposure because parts of the job are digital and information-heavy: researching records, processing measurement data, creating maps with GIS/CAD, and preparing reports can all be increasingly assisted by AI. But the occupation still depends heavily on fieldwork, physical measurement on site, travel, and licensed legal judgment about boundaries, which limits full automation.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
rationale: This job is heavily centered on digital information processing: reviewing tax returns, analyzing financial records, applying tax rules, documenting cases, and communicating findings. AI can automate or greatly accelerate much of this analytical and paperwork-heavy work, though some exposure is capped by the need for legal judgment, taxpayer interaction, investigations, and occasional field audits.

title: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs
rationale: This job requires continuous physical presence in a vehicle, real-world navigation, passenger assistance, and in-person customer service, which keeps exposure below the highest levels. But the core task of driving is a major target for AI and autonomy, so over time self-driving systems and AI dispatching could substantially reshape demand for human drivers.

title: Teacher assistants
rationale: Teacher assistants’ core work is in-person supervision, behavior support, individualized help, and in many cases physical assistance with children or students with disabilities, which limits direct automation. AI can still meaningfully affect lesson reinforcement, grading support, recordkeeping, and creation of educational materials, but it is more likely to augment than replace most day-to-day duties.

title: Technical writers
rationale: Technical writing is almost entirely digital work: drafting, revising, summarizing, standardizing, and formatting documentation are all tasks where AI is improving rapidly and can already provide major assistance. Human input still matters for understanding complex products, coordinating with engineers, and ensuring accuracy and usability, but AI is likely to substantially increase productivity and reduce labor demand per unit of output.

title: Telecommunications technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is physical: installing, testing, repairing, climbing towers, running cable, and working in homes, businesses, or central offices. AI may help with diagnostics, remote monitoring alerts, documentation, and troubleshooting guidance, but it does not replace the hands-on fieldwork, safety demands, and on-site customer interaction that define the occupation.

title: Tellers
rationale: Tellers’ work includes highly routine, rules-based transaction processing and customer service tasks that are increasingly being automated through ATMs, kiosks, online banking, fraud detection, and remote service tools. The job still requires physical presence to handle cash and interact with customers, but AI and related automation can significantly reduce headcount by shifting routine transactions away from human tellers and making each remaining teller more productive.

title: Top executives
rationale: Top executives do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing reports, reviewing budgets, setting strategy, communicating, and coordinating decisions—so AI can meaningfully augment much of their information processing and planning. But the core of the job also depends on leadership, accountability, negotiation, trust, and high-stakes judgment across real organizations, which limits full automation even as AI may reduce the need for some managerial layers.

title: Tour and travel guides
rationale: The core of this job is physical, place-based, and highly interpersonal: escorting groups, managing logistics in real time, and engaging visitors on-site. AI can help with peripheral tasks like research, itinerary planning, translation, marketing, and recordkeeping, but it is unlikely to replace the in-person presence, adaptability, and customer interaction at the center of the occupation.

title: Training and development managers
rationale: This is predominantly knowledge work done through planning, evaluating materials, analyzing training needs, budgeting, and coordinating staff—areas where AI can significantly assist or boost productivity. But the role also depends heavily on managerial judgment, cross-department collaboration, leadership, and organizational relationship-building, which makes it less exposed than fully digital execution-focused jobs.

title: Training and development specialists
rationale: This job has high AI exposure because much of the core work is digital knowledge work: assessing needs, creating training materials, building online modules, reviewing content, and evaluating programs. However, exposure is not at the very top because specialists also deliver training, facilitate learning, coordinate with stakeholders, and adapt to organizational culture and live audience dynamics, which still benefit from human presence and judgment.

title: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers
rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge work—planning logistics, analyzing operations, budgeting, compliance review, and coordinating across departments—where AI can significantly boost productivity and automate parts of scheduling, reporting, forecasting, and optimization. But the role also depends heavily on overseeing physical operations, managing staff, handling disruptions in real time, and maintaining safety and service in warehouses and transportation networks, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Travel agents
rationale: Travel agents’ core work is largely digital information processing: researching options, comparing fares, building itineraries, communicating with clients, and making bookings on computer systems. AI can already assist heavily with trip planning, recommendations, customer communication, and routine reservation tasks, though human judgment, sales ability, and handling complex disruptions still keep the role from being a 10.

title: Tutors
rationale: Tutoring combines knowledge work that AI is increasingly good at—explaining concepts, generating practice materials, giving feedback, and helping with test prep—with live human interaction and adaptation to individual students. AI will likely automate or commoditize many routine tutoring tasks and reduce demand in some segments, but human presence, motivation, rapport, and specialized support for diverse learners keep exposure below the highest levels.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
rationale: This job’s core work happens in person, in real time, on the field or court, requiring physical presence, positioning, visual observation, and split-second judgment under pressure. AI may assist at the margins through video replay, training, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the core officiating role broadly in the near term.

title: Urban and regional planners
rationale: Urban and regional planners do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing data, evaluating site plans, using GIS, and writing reports—so AI can meaningfully accelerate core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends heavily on stakeholder meetings, public communication, political judgment, and occasional site visits, which limits full automation even as AI raises productivity.

title: Veterinarians
rationale: Veterinarians have meaningful AI exposure in diagnostic support, imaging interpretation, recordkeeping, treatment planning, and client communication, all of which can improve productivity. But the core of the job still requires physical examination, surgery, procedures, emergency response, and in-person judgment with animals and owners, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Veterinary assistants and laboratory animal caretakers
rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: feeding, bathing, restraining animals, cleaning cages, sterilizing equipment, and assisting with procedures. AI may help a bit with recordkeeping, scheduling, or monitoring data, but it does not meaningfully automate the hands-on animal care and real-time physical assistance that define the occupation.

title: Veterinary technologists and technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and in-person: restraining animals, administering treatments and anesthesia, collecting samples, assisting in surgery, and providing hands-on care. AI can help with documentation, case histories, lab interpretation, imaging support, and owner communication, but it is unlikely to replace the manual, clinical, and animal-handling parts that dominate the occupation.

title: Waiters and waitresses
rationale: This job’s core work is physical and in-person: greeting diners, taking orders, carrying food, checking on tables, and handling the live restaurant experience. AI can affect peripheral and adjacent tasks through kiosks, digital ordering, and payment automation, but it does not replace most of the hands-on service and real-time customer interaction.

title: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators
rationale: This job has meaningful exposure because operators already use computerized control systems, and AI can further automate monitoring, anomaly detection, process optimization, and regulatory reporting. But the core occupation still requires on-site physical presence for inspections, sampling, maintenance, safety response, and manual operation during malfunctions or emergencies, which limits overall exposure.

title: Water transportation workers
rationale: This work is centered on physically operating, maintaining, and supervising vessels in real-world marine environments, often requiring onboard presence, manual skill, and safety-critical judgment. AI may assist with navigation, monitoring, maintenance diagnostics, and paperwork, but it is unlikely to replace the core hands-on and in-person duties for most water transportation workers in the near term.

title: Web developers and digital designers
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: writing code, designing interfaces, creating mockups, testing usability, and monitoring sites are all tasks AI can increasingly perform or accelerate. Human judgment, client communication, and product taste still matter, but AI is likely to substantially reshape workflows and raise productivity enough to reduce labor needed per project.

title: Welders, cutters, solderers, and brazers
rationale: This occupation is dominated by physical, hands-on work in factories, construction sites, and repair settings, requiring manual dexterity, spatial judgment, and real-time control of equipment around hazardous materials. AI may help with blueprint interpretation, quality inspection, or increase automation in some manufacturing settings, but it does not strongly affect the core in-person welding and cutting tasks for most workers.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
rationale: AI can automate and augment meaningful parts of this job—lead generation, customer research, drafting outreach, answering routine product questions, CRM updates, reporting, and some inside-sales interactions. But much of the core value still depends on human relationship-building, persuasion, negotiation, travel, and in-person customer contact, especially for complex or technical products, so exposure is high but not near-maximum.

title: Wind turbine technicians
rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical and site-specific: climbing turbines, inspecting equipment, troubleshooting mechanical/electrical systems, and making repairs in dangerous outdoor environments. AI may help with remote monitoring, diagnostics, scheduling, and documentation, but it does not replace the hands-on maintenance, physical dexterity, and on-site safety work that define the occupation.

title: Woodworkers
rationale: Woodworking is centered on physical machine operation, material handling, assembly, finishing, and in-person safety awareness, which limits direct AI substitution. AI may help with design interpretation, CNC programming, quality checks, and productivity in automated shops, but the core work remains hands-on and tied to physical equipment and materials.

title: Writers and authors
rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, drafting, revising, and producing text can largely be done from a computer anywhere. AI is rapidly improving at core writing tasks such as generating copy, summarizing research, adapting tone, and iterating drafts, so it is likely to both automate some work directly and sharply increase output per writer, even though top-tier creativity, original reporting, and client/audience judgment still preserve some human role.

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with the occupation’s digital tasks—data analysis, modeling, GIS, literature review, and drafting reports or research papers. But a large share of the job still depends on fieldwork, specimen collection, observing animals in natural habitats, and context-specific scientific judgment in the physical world, which limits full automation.
